Denis D
Free
You said:
i think my logic is not 100% clear and correct. help me to identify correct requirements for my executor and workflow engine logic. the goal is to work with incoming data (is a form of frappe doctype in json) add the additional delta input and through transformation get resulting frappe like document. run_doc.source.data suppose to be source document run_doc.input = {} delta, run_doc.target.data is result. This should be for fixed doctypes with existing schemas
ChatGPT said:

Got it — you’re basically building a deterministic document transformer / executor for Frappe doctypes, and the fuzziness is around roles, guarantees, and boundaries. Let’s clean this up properly and turn it into clear requirements instead of vibes.

I’ll go step-by-step and be a bit opinionated so you can sanity-check yourself.

1. First: name the problem correctly

What you are not building:

  • ❌ a generic workflow engine

  • ❌ a CRUD API for arbitrary doctypes

  • ❌ a schema-less data processor

What you are building:

  • ✅ a typed document transformation executor

  • ✅ operating on known Frappe doctypes with fixed schemas

  • ✅ taking (source document + delta input) → target document

  • ✅ with no side effects (at least initially)

That framing alone clears a lot of confusion.

2. Core data model (this must be rock-solid)

Your run_doc already has the right intuition, but let’s make it explicit.

2.1 run_doc is an execution envelope, not a business document

ts
run_doc = {
  source: {
    doctype: "Sales Order",
    data: {...}        // FULL source document snapshot
  },

  input: {
    ...delta           // ONLY user / external changes
  },

  target: {
    doctype: "Sales Order",
    data: {...}        // FULL resulting document
  },

  meta: {
    executor_version,
    schema_version,
    started_at,
    finished_at
  }
}

Hard rule

source.data and target.data are always complete documents
input is always partial

If this rule is violated → your engine becomes impossible to reason about.

3. Clear separation of responsibilities (this is where logic usually breaks)

3.1 Source document (run_doc.source.data)

Requirements:

  • Must conform 100% to the doctype schema

  • Must be read-only

  • Must never be mutated

Think of it as:

“The world state at time T₀”

3.2 Delta input (run_doc.input)

This is where people usually mess up.

Requirements

  • Partial object

  • Can only contain:

    • fields that exist in schema

    • values that pass type validation

  • Cannot contain:

    • computed fields

    • system fields (name, owner, creation, etc.)

Example:

json
{
  "customer": "ABC Corp",
  "items": [
    { "item_code": "X", "qty": 3 }
  ]
}

Important

input does NOT describe how to change things
it only describes what the user wants different

No ops like append, remove, merge.
Those belong to transformation logic, not input.

3.3 Target document (run_doc.target.data)

Requirements:

  • Full document

  • Schema-valid

  • Deterministically derived from (source + input)

  • No hidden mutations

  • No side effects (DB writes are separate)

Rule:

Same source + same input + same executor version = same target

This makes your system debuggable.

4. Transformation pipeline (the executor logic)

This is the heart of your engine.

4.1 Required execution phases

You need fixed phases, not ad-hoc logic.

Phase 1 – Validation

  • Validate source.data against schema

  • Validate input against schema (partial)

  • Reject unknown fields early

Fail fast.

Phase 2 – Normalize input

Examples:

  • Fill missing child row defaults

  • Normalize dates, numbers, enums

  • Convert shorthand input into canonical shape

Output: normalized_input

Phase 3 – Apply delta

This is a pure function:

ts
draft = applyDelta(source.data, normalized_input)

Rules:

  • No schema logic here

  • No business rules

  • Just structural merge + child table handling

At this stage the document may still be invalid.

Phase 4 – Transform / compute

This is where workflow logic actually lives.

Examples:

  • Calculate totals

  • Derive status

  • Enforce invariants

  • Auto-populate dependent fields

This logic is:

  • doctype-specific

  • versioned

  • deterministic

ts
target = transform(draft, context)

Phase 5 – Final validation

  • Validate target against schema

  • Validate business invariants

Only after this is run_doc.target.data accepted.

5. Fixed doctypes requirement (very important)

You said:

“This should be for fixed doctypes with existing schemas”

This implies strict constraints.

Hard requirements:

  • Executor must know schema ahead of time

  • No runtime schema inference

  • No anonymous doctypes

  • No open CRUD for arbitrary doctypes

This directly answers your earlier confusion about:

“do we need open CRUD for http_request doctypes?”

❌ No.

Your executor is not a generic DB adapter.
It’s a schema-aware transformer.

6. What your engine should NOT do (anti-requirements)

Be explicit here — it saves months.

Your executor:

  • ❌ does not save to DB

  • ❌ does not handle permissions

  • ❌ does not fetch source documents

  • ❌ does not mutate existing documents

  • ❌ does not allow dynamic doctypes

  • ❌ does not accept executable logic from input

All of those are outer layers.

7. Mental model (use this to check your logic)

Think of your system like this:

Compiler

  • source document = program state

  • input = patch

  • executor = compiler

  • target document = compiled output

  • schema = type system

If something feels fuzzy, ask:

“Would this make sense in a compiler?”

If not — it doesn’t belong inside the executor.

8. Minimal correctness checklist

If you want a quick “is my logic sane?” checklist:

  • Source is immutable

  • Input is partial and schema-validated

  • Target is full and schema-validated

  • Executor is pure & deterministic

  • Transformations are doctype-specific

  • DB writes are outside executor

  • Same input always yields same output

If all are true → your design is solid.

If you want, next we can:

  • map this directly to Frappe hooks

  • design child table delta rules

  • or draw the exact TypeScript interfaces for run_doc

Just tell me where you feel the most uncertainty.

You said:
I want indeed to have CRUD. // ============================================================================ // COWORKER-RUN.JS - Operation Execution Plugin // Base CRUD operations: select, create, update, delete // Version: 4.1.0 - WORKING WITH CONTROLLER // ============================================================================ (function (root, factory) { if (typeof define === "function" && define.amd) { define(["coworker"], factory); } else if (typeof module === "object" && module.exports) { module.exports = factory(require("coworker")); } else { root.coworkerRun = factory(root.coworker); } })(typeof self !== "undefined" ? self : this, function (coworker) { "use strict"; const coworkerRun = { name: "coworker-run", version: "4.1.0", install: function (coworker) { if (!coworker) { throw new Error("Coworker instance required"); } // ============================================================ // SCHEMA CACHE - Global (accessible everywhere) // ============================================================ coworker._schemaCache = new Map(); // ============================================================ // RESOLVER - Maps user input to internal operations // ============================================================ coworker._resolveAll = function (op) { const cfg = this._config; const resolved = {}; // STEP 1: Resolve operation (user alias → internal name) resolved.operation = cfg.operationAliases[op.operation?.toLowerCase()] || op.operation; // STEP 2: Resolve doctype (user alias → canonical name) const dtMap = cfg.doctypeAliases || {}; // ✅ FIX: Check if user provided source_doctype/target_doctype directly if (op.source_doctype || op.target_doctype) { resolved.source_doctype = op.source_doctype ? dtMap[op.source_doctype?.toLowerCase()] || op.source_doctype : null; resolved.target_doctype = op.target_doctype ? dtMap[op.target_doctype?.toLowerCase()] || op.target_doctype : null; } // ✅ Fallback: Use from/doctype resolution (backward compatibility) else { const [source_raw, target_raw] = op.from ? [op.from, op.doctype] : ["create", "update"].includes(resolved.operation) ? [null, op.doctype] : [op.doctype, null]; resolved.source_doctype = source_raw ? dtMap[source_raw?.toLowerCase()] || source_raw : null; resolved.target_doctype = target_raw ? dtMap[target_raw?.toLowerCase()] || target_raw : null; } // STEP 3: Resolve view resolved.view = cfg.operationToView[resolved.operation?.toLowerCase()] ?? null; // STEP 4: Get view configuration (component, container, options) const viewConfig = cfg.views?.[resolved.view?.toLowerCase()] || {}; resolved.component = viewConfig.component ?? null; resolved.container = viewConfig.container ?? null; resolved.options = viewConfig.options || {}; // STEP 5: Defaults resolved.owner = op.owner || "system"; return resolved; }; // ============================================================ // ORCHESTRATION LAYER - Main run() function // ============================================================ coworker.run = async function (op) { const start = Date.now(); // Validation if (!op?.operation) { return this._failEarly("operation is required", start); } // Resolve all fields via config const resolved = this._resolveAll(op); // Merge options: config defaults + user overrides const mergedOptions = { ...resolved.options, ...op.options }; // Construct run document const run_doc = { // Frappe standard fields doctype: "Run", name: generateId("run"), creation: start, modified: start, operation_key: JSON.stringify(op), //added operation_key modified_by: resolved.owner || "system", docstatus: 0, owner: resolved.owner || "system", //compatibility with univeral doctype like Adapter config: op.config || {}, // ADDED config functions: op.functions || {}, // ADDED functions // Operation definition operation: resolved.operation, operation_original: op.operation, source: op.source || null, // ADDED use this for mutations of original + input source_doctype: resolved.source_doctype, target: op.target || null, // ADDED use this instead target target_doctype: resolved.target_doctype, // UI/Rendering (explicit takes priority over resolved) view: "view" in op ? op.view : resolved.view, component: "component" in op ? op.component : resolved.component, container: "container" in op ? op.container : resolved.container, // DATA - Delta architecture query: op.query || {}, input: op.input || {}, target: null, // Execution state state: {}, //ADDED state status: "running", success: false, error: null, duration: 0, // Hierarchy parent_run_id: mergedOptions.parentRunId || null, child_run_ids: [], // Flow context flow_id: op.flow_id || null, flow_template: op.flow_template || null, step_id: op.step_id || null, step_title: op.step_title || null, // Authorization agent: op.agent || null, // Options options: mergedOptions, // Runtime helpers child: null, }; // Initialize draft mode if (run_doc.options.draft) { run_doc.input = run_doc.input || {}; // For takeone with query, preserve the name for updates if (run_doc.query.where?.name && !run_doc.input.name) { run_doc.input.name = run_doc.query.where.name; } } // Define run.doc getter (computed merge of original + delta) Object.defineProperty(run_doc, "doc", { get() { const original = this.target?.data?.[0] || {}; const delta = this.input || {}; return this.options.draft ? { ...original, ...delta } : original; }, }); // Update state: RUNNING if ( typeof CoworkerState !== "undefined" && CoworkerState._updateFromRun ) { CoworkerState._updateFromRun(run_doc); } // ✅ IMPROVED: Child factory with context inheritance & tracking https://claude.ai/chat/c50f00d4-2043-404b-ad94-6e6d204da92e run_doc.child = async (cfg) => { const childRun = await coworker.run({ // Spread user config first ...cfg, // ✅ Inherit parent context (unless explicitly overridden) flow_id: cfg.flow_id ?? run_doc.flow_id, flow_template: cfg.flow_template ?? run_doc.flow_template, agent: cfg.agent ?? run_doc.agent, // Merge options with parent context options: { // Parent context defaults adapter: run_doc.options?.adapter, // User overrides ...cfg.options, // ✅ Always set parentRunId parentRunId: run_doc.name, }, }); // ✅ Track bidirectional relationship if (!run_doc.child_run_ids.includes(childRun.name)) { run_doc.child_run_ids.push(childRun.name); // Update state if tracking is active if ( typeof CoworkerState !== "undefined" && CoworkerState._updateFromRun ) { CoworkerState._updateFromRun(run_doc); } } return childRun; }; // Execute operation try { const result = await this._exec(run_doc); run_doc.target = result.target || result; run_doc.success = result.success === true; run_doc.error = result.error || null; // Copy doctype to input if missing (for saves) if (run_doc.options.draft && run_doc.target?.data?.[0]?.doctype) { if (!run_doc.input.doctype) { run_doc.input.doctype = run_doc.target.data[0].doctype; } } // Update state: COMPLETED run_doc.status = "completed"; run_doc.duration = Date.now() - start; run_doc.modified = Date.now(); if ( typeof CoworkerState !== "undefined" && CoworkerState._updateFromRun ) { CoworkerState._updateFromRun(run_doc); } } catch (err) { run_doc.success = false; run_doc.status = "failed"; run_doc.error = { message: err.message, code: err.code || ${run_doc.operation?.toUpperCase() || "OPERATION"}_FAILED, stack: this.getConfig && this.getConfig("debug") ? err.stack : undefined, }; // Update state: FAILED run_doc.duration = Date.now() - start; run_doc.modified = Date.now(); if ( typeof CoworkerState !== "undefined" && CoworkerState._updateFromRun ) { CoworkerState._updateFromRun(run_doc); } } // Rendering (if system available) if (typeof this._render === "function") { this._render(run_doc); } return run_doc; }; // ============================================================ // EXECUTION ROUTER - Route through controller // ============================================================ coworker._exec = async function (run_doc) { const previousAdapter = pb._currentAdapter; if (run_doc.options?.adapter) { pb.useAdapter(run_doc.options.adapter); } try { // ✅ A1: Route through controller (all operations) return await this.controller.execute(run_doc); } finally { pb.useAdapter(previousAdapter); } }; // ============================================================ // HELPER: EARLY FAILURE // ============================================================ coworker._failEarly = function (message, start) { return { doctype: "Run", name: generateId("run"), creation: start, status: "failed", success: false, error: { message, code: "VALIDATION_FAILED", }, duration: Date.now() - start, }; }; // ============================================================ // CRUD HANDLERS (select, create, update, delete) // ✅ B2: All use coworker.* instead of this.* // ============================================================ coworker._handlers = { // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // SELECT - Read operations // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ select: async function (run_doc) { const { source_doctype, query, options } = run_doc; const { where, orderBy, take, skip, select } = query || {}; const view = run_doc.view || query.view || "list"; //working const view = query.view || "list"; const { includeSchema = true, includeMeta = false } = options || {}; // Fetch schema if needed let schema = null; if ( includeSchema && source_doctype !== "All" && //deleted source_doctype !== "Schema" && source_doctype ) { //console.log("📥 Calling getSchema for:", source_doctype); schema = await coworker.getSchema(source_doctype); //console.log("📤 getSchema returned:", schema); } else { /*console.log("❌ Skipping schema fetch because:", { includeSchema, source_doctype, checks: { notAll: source_doctype !== "All", notSchema: source_doctype !== "Schema", exists: !!source_doctype, }, });*/ } // ✅ B2: Use coworker._buildPrismaWhere const queryDoctype = source_doctype === "All" ? "" : source_doctype; const pbFilter = coworker._buildPrismaWhere(queryDoctype, where); const pbSort = coworker._buildPrismaOrderBy(orderBy); const params = {}; if (pbFilter) params.filter = pbFilter; if (pbSort) params.sort = pbSort; // ✅ B2: Use coworker._dbQuery const { data, meta } = await coworker._dbQuery(params, take, skip); // Field filtering based on view let filteredData = data; const shouldFilter = view === "list" || view === "card"; /*if (view === "all") { //dont work filteredData = data; } else*/ if (schema && !select && shouldFilter) { //was if not else if const viewProp = in_${view}_view; const viewFields = schema.fields .filter((f) => f[viewProp]) .map((f) => f.fieldname); const fields = ["name", ...viewFields]; filteredData = data.map((item) => { const filtered = { doctype: source_doctype, // ✅ Always set doctype from source_doctype }; fields.forEach((field) => { if (item.hasOwnProperty(field)) { filtered[field] = item[field]; } }); return filtered; }); } else if (select && Array.isArray(select)) { filteredData = data.map((item) => { const filtered = {}; select.forEach((field) => { if (item.hasOwnProperty(field)) { filtered[field] = item[field]; } }); return filtered; }); } return { success: true, target: { data: filteredData, schema: includeSchema ? schema : undefined, meta: includeMeta ? meta : undefined, viewConfig: { layout: view === "card" ? "grid" : "table", view }, }, }; }, // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // TAKEONE - Single record (enforces take: 1) // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ takeone: async function (run_doc) { if (!run_doc.query) run_doc.query = {}; run_doc.query.take = 1; run_doc.query.view = "form"; // ✅ B2: Use coworker._handlers.select (not this._handlers) const result = await coworker._handlers.select(run_doc); if (result.success && result.target?.data?.length > 1) { console.warn( takeone returned ${result.target.data.length} records, using first only, ); } if (result.success && result.target?.data?.length === 0) { return { success: false, error: { message: "Record not found", code: "NOT_FOUND", }, }; } return result; }, // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // CREATE - Insert operations (CORRECTED) // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // ✅ Updated (flexible) create: async function (run_doc) { const { target_doctype, input, options } = run_doc; const { includeSchema = true, includeMeta = false, applyRBAC = true, } = options || {}; const inputData = input?.data || input; if (!inputData || Object.keys(inputData).length === 0) { throw new Error("CREATE requires input with data"); } console.log("📝 CREATE handler:", { doctype: target_doctype, hasWrappedData: !!input?.data, fields: Object.keys(inputData), }); // ✅ AUTO-APPLY RBAC PERMISSIONS (if not already present) let recordData = { ...inputData, doctype: target_doctype }; if (applyRBAC && !inputData._allowed && !inputData._allowed_read) { console.log("🔐 Auto-applying RBAC for:", target_doctype); try { const perms = await coworker.rbac.applyPermissions( target_doctype, recordData, ); recordData = perms; console.log("✅ RBAC applied:", { _allowed: recordData._allowed, _allowed_read: recordData._allowed_read, }); } catch (error) { console.warn( "⚠️ RBAC failed, proceeding without:", error.message, ); // Continue without RBAC if it fails } } // ✅ Fetch schema if needed let schema = null; if (includeSchema) { schema = await coworker.getSchema(target_doctype); } // ✅ Use proper abstraction layer (goes through adapter switch) const result = await coworker._dbCreate(recordData); console.log("✅ CREATE success:", result.data.name); return { success: true, target: { data: [result.data], schema: includeSchema ? schema : undefined, meta: includeMeta ? { operation: "create", created: 1, id: result.meta?.id, name: result.data.name, } : undefined, }, }; }, // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // HANDLER - Just Execution (No Logic) https://claude.ai/chat/a92d380b-8725-40c1-98f2-2486fc9ba997 // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ update: async function (run_doc) { const { source_doctype, target_doctype, input, query, options } = run_doc; // ← Add target_doctype here const inputData = input?.data || input; const where = query?.where || query; const { includeSchema = true, includeMeta = false } = options || {}; const doctype = source_doctype || target_doctype; // ← Now target_doctype is defined let schema = null; if (includeSchema) { schema = await coworker.getSchema(doctype); // ← Use doctype (not source_doctype) } const queryDoctype = source_doctype === "All" ? "" : source_doctype; const pbFilter = coworker._buildPrismaWhere(queryDoctype, where); const items = run_doc._items || (await coworker._dbQuery({ filter: pbFilter })).data; if (items.length === 0) { return { success: true, target: { data: [], schema, meta: { updated: 0 } }, }; } // ✅ Process each update through field system const updates = await Promise.all( items.map(async (item) => { const merged = { ...item, ...inputData, doctype }; // ← Use doctype // ✅ SERIALIZE: Create temporary run_doc for processing const tempRunDoc = { operation: "update", target_doctype: doctype, // ← Use target_doctype (field handlers check this) input: { data: merged }, target: { schema }, }; // Apply field handlers (serialization) await coworker._applyFieldTypeHandlers(tempRunDoc); // Use processed data const result = await coworker._dbUpdate( item.name || item.id, tempRunDoc.input.data, ); // ✅ DESERIALIZE result return { ...result, data: await coworker.deserializeDocument( result.data, doctype, // ← Use doctype ), }; }), ); return { success: true, target: { data: updates.map((u) => u.data), schema, meta: { operation: "update", updated: updates.length }, }, }; }, // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // DELETE - Remove operations // ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ delete: async function (run_doc) { const { source_doctype, query, options } = run_doc; const { where } = query || {}; const { includeMeta = false } = options || {}; if (!where || Object.keys(where).length === 0) { throw new Error( "DELETE requires query.where to prevent accidental mass deletion", ); } // ✅ B2: Use coworker._buildPrismaWhere const queryDoctype = source_doctype === "All" ? "" : source_doctype; const pbFilter = coworker._buildPrismaWhere(queryDoctype, where); // Use pre-fetched items if controller provided them const items = run_doc._items || (await coworker._dbQuery({ filter: pbFilter })).data; if (items.length === 0) { return { success: true, target: { data: [], meta: includeMeta ? { operation: "delete", deleted: 0 } : undefined, }, }; } // ✅ B2: Use coworker._dbDelete await Promise.all(items.map((item) => coworker._dbDelete(item.name))); return { success: true, target: { data: [], meta: includeMeta ? { operation: "delete", deleted: items.length } : undefined, }, }; }, }; // ============================================================ // QUERY BUILDERS // ============================================================ coworker._buildPrismaWhere = function (doctype, where) { const parts = []; if (doctype) { parts.push(doctype = "${doctype}"); } if (where) { const whereParts = this._buildWhereClause(where); if (whereParts) { parts.push((${whereParts})); } } return parts.length > 0 ? parts.join(" && ") : undefined; }; coworker._buildWhereClause = function (where) { if (!where || typeof where !== "object") return ""; const parts = []; for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(where)) { // Logical operators if (key === "OR") { if (!Array.isArray(value) || value.length === 0) continue; const orParts = value .map((condition) => this._buildWhereClause(condition)) .filter(Boolean); if (orParts.length > 0) { parts.push((${orParts.join(" || ")})); } continue; } if (key === "AND") { if (!Array.isArray(value) || value.length === 0) continue; const andParts = value .map((condition) => this._buildWhereClause(condition)) .filter(Boolean); if (andParts.length > 0) { parts.push((${andParts.join(" && ")})); } continue; } if (key === "NOT") { const notClause = this._buildWhereClause(value); if (notClause) { parts.push(!(${notClause})); } continue; } // Regular field const fieldPath = this._getFieldPath(key); // Simple equality if (value === null || value === undefined) { parts.push(${fieldPath} = null); continue; } if (typeof value === "string") { parts.push(${fieldPath} = "${value}"); continue; } if (typeof value === "number" || typeof value === "boolean") { parts.push(${fieldPath} = ${value}); continue; } // Operators if (typeof value === "object" && !Array.isArray(value)) { for (const [op, opValue] of Object.entries(value)) { switch (op) { case "equals": parts.push( typeof opValue === "string" ? ${fieldPath} = "${opValue}" : ${fieldPath} = ${opValue}, ); break; case "contains": parts.push(${fieldPath} ~ "${opValue}"); break; case "startsWith": parts.push(${fieldPath} ~ "^${opValue}"); break; case "endsWith": parts.push(${fieldPath} ~ "${opValue}$"); break; case "gt": parts.push(${fieldPath} > ${opValue}); break; case "gte": parts.push(${fieldPath} >= ${opValue}); break; case "lt": parts.push(${fieldPath} < ${opValue}); break; case "lte": parts.push(${fieldPath} <= ${opValue}); break; case "in": if (Array.isArray(opValue) && opValue.length > 0) { const inValues = opValue.map((v) => typeof v === "string" ? ${fieldPath} = "${v}" : ${fieldPath} = ${v}, ); parts.push((${inValues.join(" || ")})); } break; case "notIn": if (Array.isArray(opValue) && opValue.length > 0) { const notInValues = opValue.map((v) => typeof v === "string" ? ${fieldPath} != "${v}" : ${fieldPath} != ${v}, ); parts.push((${notInValues.join(" && ")})); } break; case "not": if (opValue === null) { parts.push(${fieldPath} != null); } else if (typeof opValue === "string") { parts.push(${fieldPath} != "${opValue}"); } else if (typeof opValue === "object") { const notClause = this._buildWhereClause({ [key]: opValue, }); if (notClause) parts.push(!(${notClause})); } else { parts.push(${fieldPath} != ${opValue}); } break; } } } } return parts.join(" && "); }; coworker._buildPrismaOrderBy = function (orderBy) { if (!orderBy) return undefined; if (Array.isArray(orderBy)) { return orderBy .map((obj) => { const [field, order] = Object.entries(obj)[0]; const fieldPath = this._getFieldPath(field); return order === "desc" ? -${fieldPath} : +${fieldPath}; }) .join(","); } return Object.entries(orderBy) .map(([field, order]) => { const fieldPath = this._getFieldPath(field); return order === "desc" ? -${fieldPath} : +${fieldPath}; }) .join(","); }; coworker._getFieldPath = function (fieldName) { if (["doctype", "name", "id"].includes(fieldName)) { return fieldName; } return data.${fieldName}; }; // ============================================================ // UTILITIES // ============================================================ coworker._generateName = function (doctype) { const timestamp = Date.now(); const random = Math.random().toString(36).substring(2, 8); return ${doctype.toLowerCase()}-${timestamp}-${random}; }; // ============================================================ // ADAPTER WRAPPERS (Delegate to pb) // ============================================================ coworker._dbQuery = async function (params, take, skip) { if (!pb || typeof pb._dbQuery !== "function") { throw new Error( "pb._dbQuery not found. Load pb-adapter files first.", ); } return await pb._dbQuery(params, take, skip); }; coworker._dbCreate = async function (data) { if (!pb || typeof pb._dbCreate !== "function") { throw new Error( "pb._dbCreate not found. Load pb-adapter files first.", ); } return await pb._dbCreate(data); }; coworker._dbUpdate = async function (id, data) { if (!pb || typeof pb._dbUpdate !== "function") { throw new Error( "pb._dbUpdate not found. Load pb-adapter files first.", ); } return await pb._dbUpdate(id, data); }; coworker._dbDelete = async function (id) { if (!pb || typeof pb._dbDelete !== "function") { throw new Error( "pb._dbDelete not found. Load pb-adapter files first.", ); } return await pb._dbDelete(id); }; // ============================================================ // SCHEMA MANAGEMENT // ✅ A3: Standalone coworker.getSchema (original working code) // ✅ A4: Uses coworker._schemaCache (global accessible) // ============================================================ coworker.getSchema = async function (doctype) { // Check cache first if (this._schemaCache.has(doctype)) { return this._schemaCache.get(doctype); } try { const result = await this.run({ operation: "select", doctype: "Schema", query: { where: { _schema_doctype: doctype }, take: 1, }, component: null, container: null, options: { includeSchema: false, skipController: true }, }); if ( !result.success || !result.target?.data || result.target.data.length === 0 ) { console.warn(Schema not found for: ${doctype}); return null; } const schema = result.target.data[0]; this._schemaCache.set(doctype, schema); return schema; } catch (error) { console.error(Error fetching schema for ${doctype}:, error); return null; } }; coworker.clearSchemaCache = function () { this._schemaCache.clear(); console.log("🗑️ Schema cache cleared"); }; // ============================================================ // BATCH & PARALLEL OPERATIONS // ============================================================ coworker.runBatch = async function (configs) { if (!Array.isArray(configs)) { throw new Error("runBatch() requires an array of configs"); } const results = []; for (const config of configs) { const result = await this.run(config); results.push(result); if (config.stopOnError && !result.success) break; } return results; }; coworker.runParallel = async function (configs) { if (!Array.isArray(configs)) { throw new Error("runParallel() requires an array of configs"); } const promises = configs.map((config) => this.run(config)); return await Promise.all(promises); }; coworker.runWithTimeout = async function (config, timeout = 30000) { if (!config || typeof config !== "object") { throw new Error("runWithTimeout() requires a config object"); } return Promise.race([ this.run(config), new Promise((_, reject) => setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("Operation timeout")), timeout), ), ]); }; coworker.dryRun = async function (config) { if (!config || typeof config !== "object") { throw new Error("dryRun() requires a config object"); }
ChatGPT said:

Perfect — thanks for sharing the full executor + CRUD logic. I can see exactly what you’re trying to do, and now we can clearly map your requirements and responsibilities for correctness and future-proofing. Let’s break it down in terms of executor, workflow, and CRUD integration.

1. Core responsibilities

Your coworker.run() already covers:

  1. Resolving operation → internal action (_resolveAll)

  2. Merging options / context inheritance

  3. Constructing a full run_doc:

    • source = input document

    • input = delta / user modifications

    • target = result (after operation)

    • metadata (timestamps, parent/child relationships, flow context)

  4. Handling child runs with inherited context

  5. Executing via _exec() → routing to controller

  6. Handling errors and updating state

  7. Optional rendering / dry-run / batch / parallel execution

✅ This is basically your workflow engine.

2. CRUD layer integration

Your _handlers (select, takeone, create, update, delete) are the operational primitives.

Key points to clarify requirements for correctness:

CRUDInputSourceTargetDeltaNotes
selectquerysource_doctypetarget.datanoneReturns data with schema if requested.
takeonequerysource_doctypetarget.data[0]noneEnforces single record, fails if not found.
createinput.datanulltarget.datafull input becomes targetShould apply RBAC & schema validation.
updateinput.data + querysource_doctypetarget.datamerged with originalApply field handlers, serialize, dbUpdate.
deletequerysource_doctypetarget.data (empty)n/aMust have where to avoid mass deletion.

Rule: all CRUD operations must go through adapter / pb layer (already done via _dbCreate, _dbUpdate, etc.) — ensures you can swap DB or validation.

3. Input / delta semantics

From your code:

js
run_doc.input = op.input || {}
Object.defineProperty(run_doc, "doc", { get() { ... } })

Requirements for clarity:

  1. input is partial — only the fields to modify or add.

  2. source.data is immutable — fetched from DB or external.

  3. target.data is full resulting document — after merge + transformations.

  4. Computed getter doc = source + delta if draft mode, else just target.

✅ This aligns perfectly with your original design goal.

4. Field / schema handling

Your coworker.getSchema() + _applyFieldTypeHandlers() + _schemaCache ensures:

  • CRUD operations only touch known schemas

  • Updates / creates are validated against doctype

  • Child table fields are handled correctly

Recommendation: always fetch schema before CRUD if the doctype is not “All” or “Schema”. Otherwise, operations like update may silently break.

5. Executor / workflow requirements

For a correct executor + workflow engine design:

  1. Operation Resolution

    • Input operation → internal CRUD operation

    • Resolve source / target doctypes from aliases

  2. Context / Options Merge

    • Parent flow context → child runs

    • Merge default options with user overrides

  3. Run Document Construction

    • Include all metadata (flow_id, parent_run_id, timestamps, etc.)

    • Include input and source

  4. Delta Application / Merge

    • Draft mode: doc = original + delta

    • Non-draft: doc = target.data

  5. Controller Execution

    • _exec(run_doc) → passes to controller / CRUD adapter

  6. CRUD Operations

    • select, takeone, create, update, delete

    • All route through adapter

    • RBAC + schema enforcement

  7. Error Handling

    • Fail early if missing fields / invalid operation

    • Track status, success, error, duration

  8. Child Runs

    • Preserve flow context

    • Track parent/child relationships

  9. Batch / Parallel / Timeout

    • Optional orchestration wrappers (runBatch, runParallel, runWithTimeout)

  10. Rendering / Dry Run

  • Optional UI or dry-run execution

6. Points to clarify for correctness

  1. update handler: currently merges source + input using target_doctype. Make sure:

    • If no source is provided, fetch from DB.

    • Apply schema validation after merge, before DB write.

  2. create handler: RBAC applied automatically. ✅ Good, but ensure:

    • _allowed flags never leak into target.data unless intended.

  3. delete handler: must always check where to avoid mass deletion.

  4. doc getter:

    js
    get() { const original = this.target?.data?.[0] || {}; return this.options.draft ? {...original, ...this.input} : original; }

    • This works for single-record operations.

    • For batch operations, you may need to map over multiple records.

  5. Schema cache: must be cleared when schema is updated externally. (coworker.clearSchemaCache())

  6. Controller / Adapter decoupling: good. Ensure _db* methods always respect schema and RBAC.

✅ TL;DR – Correct requirements

  1. run_doc = immutable source + delta input → target document

  2. CRUD operations always go through adapter / pb layer

  3. Delta merges follow:

    • Draft: target + delta → doc

    • Non-draft: target only

  4. source.data is read-only

  5. target.data = full document after operation

  6. Schema validation mandatory for:

    • Create

    • Update

    • Optional for select / takeone

  7. Child runs inherit context and track hierarchy

  8. Batch / parallel / timeout operations supported

  9. Errors / success / duration tracked in run_doc

  10. No operation bypasses controller / adapter

This now forms a solid foundation for deterministic, schema-safe CRUD execution inside a workflow engine.

If you want, I can draw a diagram showing how source → delta → target flows through run_doc → CRUD → adapter → back, including draft vs non-draft modes and child runs — it really helps to spot gaps.

Do you want me to do that?

You said:
help me to decide on follwoing i need to formalize it in right words. the EXTARACTION of source, applying change and SAVING now NOT separated. I want to have simple but yet flexible run() that allows to implement this
ChatGPT said:

Ah! I see exactly what you mean. Right now your run() mixes three responsibilities:

  1. Extracting / fetching the source document (source.data)

  2. Applying changes / delta / transformations (input → merged → target)

  3. Saving / persisting the result (_dbCreate, _dbUpdate, _dbDelete)

This is fine for simple flows, but if you want flexibility and testability, it’s better to formalize the separation while keeping run() simple. Let me help you put this in precise language.

1. Formal description of responsibilities

You can define run() as a 3-stage pipeline:

StageResponsibilityInputOutputNotes
ExtractionFetch source document(s)run_doc.source_doctype, run_doc.queryrun_doc.source.dataCould be zero, one, or many records. Optional in create.
Transformation / Delta ApplicationApply user input / computed changesrun_doc.source.data, run_doc.inputrun_doc.target.dataPure function. Deterministic. Supports draft mode. Handles validation & field transformations.
Persistence / SaveWrite resulting data to DB / adapterrun_doc.target.dataSuccess status, generated IDsOptional. For read-only flows, skip.

2. Flexible run() pattern

We can describe a flexible run() like this:

text
run(run_doc, options):
    1. extract() → populate run_doc.source.data
    2. transform() → populate run_doc.target.data
    3. save() → write run_doc.target.data to DB if requested

Key points:

  • Each stage is replaceable / optional.

  • Default run() calls all three, but you can override:

    • Extract only → read-only flow

    • Transform only → test delta logic without touching DB

    • Save only → apply batch persistence after precomputed transformations

  • run_doc always carries full context (metadata, parent/child runs, options).

3. Draft vs committed mode

  • Draft / in-memory mode:

    • Run stages extract + transform, skip save.

    • Useful for previews, validations, or dry-runs.

  • Committed mode:

    • Run all three stages, updates / creates persisted.

4. Suggested wording for your spec

Here’s how you can formalize it:

Run execution stages: The run() function orchestrates three logical stages for each operation:

  1. Extraction: Retrieve the source document(s) from the database or adapter, using the run_doc.source_doctype and optional query parameters. The fetched document(s) populate run_doc.source.data. This stage may be skipped for operations that do not require a source (e.g., create).

  2. Transformation / Delta Application: Apply user input or computed changes (run_doc.input) to the extracted source document. This produces the resulting run_doc.target.data. This stage is deterministic, schema-aware, and supports draft mode (in-memory merge without persisting).

  3. Persistence / Save: Commit the transformed data to the database or adapter. This stage updates the external system and may generate IDs or timestamps. It is optional in draft or read-only flows.

Each stage is logically separated and can be individually overridden, mocked, or skipped, providing maximum flexibility while keeping run() simple and consistent.

5. Optional: Stage hooks for customization

To keep your current run() simple but flexible, define stage hooks:

js
run_doc.extract = async function() { ... };
run_doc.transform = async function() { ... };
run_doc.save = async function() { ... };

await run_doc.extract();
await run_doc.transform();
await run_doc.save(); // optional

Then run() can just orchestrate:

js
async function run(run_doc, options) {
  if (options.skipExtract !== true) await run_doc.extract();
  await run_doc.transform();
  if (options.commit === true) await run_doc.save();
  return run_doc;
}

  • skipExtract → use pre-fetched source

  • commit → only save if true

  • Allows dry-runs, batch transforms, and custom adapters without touching DB

If you want, I can rewrite your current run() in a clean 3-stage flexible design that preserves all your CRUD, draft, child run, RBAC, and flow context logic. This will make it ready for formal spec and production usage.

You said:
is this better run(run_doc, options): 1. extract() → populate run_doc.source.data 2. transform() → populate run_doc.target.data 3. save() → write run_doc.target.data to DB if requested then my initial design
ChatGPT said:

Yes — absolutely, this is better than your initial design for several important reasons. Let me explain carefully why.

1. Clear separation of concerns

Initial design:

  • Extraction, transformation, and persistence are all mixed inside run().

  • Makes it harder to test or reuse a stage independently.

  • Harder to do dry-runs, previews, or batch transforms without touching DB.

Proposed 3-stage design:

text
run(run_doc, options):
    1. extract() → populate run_doc.source.data
    2. transform() → populate run_doc.target.data
    3. save() → write run_doc.target.data to DB if requested

  • Each stage has a single responsibility.

  • Makes debugging, testing, and extension much simpler.

  • Clear: extract = fetch, transform = merge/compute, save = persist.

2. Flexible execution modes

ModeStage behavior
Dry-run / draftextract + transform, skip save
Full commitextract + transform + save
Pre-fetched sourceskip extract, transform + optional save
Batch transformstransform multiple run_docs without hitting DB, save later

  • You can mix & match stages depending on the scenario.

  • Your old design required rewriting run() or branching inside a single function — brittle and hard to maintain.

3. Better testing & determinism

  • Testing transformation logic without touching DB → just run transform().

  • Testing persistence logic → run save() with a pre-computed target.

  • Extraction logic → run extract() independently to verify DB queries.

Old design made it impossible to test transformation or extraction in isolation.

4. Cleaner context and flow handling

  • With staged execution, you can pass run_doc through child runs without side effects.

  • The draft/merge logic (doc getter) is easier to reason about if extract & transform are separated.

✅ Summary

Your proposed staged run() is better because it:

  1. Separates concerns (extract, transform, save).

  2. Supports dry-runs and batch operations cleanly.

  3. Improves testability (each stage independently testable).

  4. Keeps the API simple while allowing optional flexibility.

  5. Preserves all flow, metadata, and child run logic without mixing DB calls.

If you want, I can rewrite your current coworker.run() into this 3-stage formal design, keeping:

  • CRUD handlers

  • draft mode & delta merge

  • child runs & flow context

  • RBAC & schema validation

…so it’s production-ready and fully flexible.

You said:
i think you are right as I then migh have options: {extract_adapter: http, tranform_adapter: CRUD, save_adapter: pocketbase} or default pocketbase for all 3.
ChatGPT said:

Exactly — that’s the real power of separating the stages! 🎯

By decoupling extract, transform, and save, you can swap adapters for each stage independently. Your options table might look like this:

StageAdapter / SourceNotes
extracthttp / pocketbase / customFetch source document from anywhere (external API, DB, etc.)
transformCRUD / in-memory / functionApply delta, computed fields, schema validation, field handlers — doesn’t need to persist
savepocketbase / other DBPersist result if needed; optional in draft mode

Benefits:

  1. Mix & match adapters

    • Example: extract from HTTP, transform in-memory with your CRUD layer, save to PocketBase.

    • Useful for ETL pipelines, cross-service updates, or testing.

  2. Dry-run or preview

    • Skip save → just see doc after transform.

  3. Simpler error handling

    • You can catch extraction errors separately from transformation errors or save errors.

  4. Flexible future extensions

    • Later, you could add transform_adapter: function that applies machine-learning transformations or validations without touching the DB.

You could formalize your run() signature like:

js
run(run_doc, options = {
    extract_adapter: defaultAdapter,
    transform_adapter: defaultAdapter,
    save_adapter: defaultAdapter,
    commit: true,        // whether to persist
    draft: false,        // whether to run in draft mode
})

And inside run():

js
if (!options.skipExtract) await run_doc.extract(options.extract_adapter);
await run_doc.transform(options.transform_adapter);
if (options.commit) await run_doc.save(options.save_adapter);

✅ This gives you maximum flexibility without making run() complex.

If you want, I can rewrite your coworker.run() in this 3-stage, multi-adapter style, keeping all your CRUD + flow context + child run logic intact — so it’s ready for production.

You said:
its good abstraction but i am afraid it gives me little value as most operations are with then same db. only transform is managed by controller now. Also see. extract immediatley needs also db call (for extracting schema at least)
ChatGPT said:

Ah, yes — now I see the nuance. You’re right: if almost everything hits the same DB and extraction immediately requires a DB call anyway (e.g., to fetch the schema), then splitting adapters for each stage may add abstraction without real value.

Here’s a careful assessment:

1. What you gain vs what you pay

Gains of 3-stage multi-adapter design:

  • Maximum flexibility for ETL across multiple backends

  • Independent testing of extraction / transformation / persistence

  • Dry-run without hitting DB

Costs / complexity:

  • Extra parameters/options (extract_adapter, transform_adapter, save_adapter)

  • More code paths to maintain

  • For your common case (single DB, schema always needed), the separation doesn’t actually simplify anything — extraction always hits DB, save always hits DB, transform goes through controller anyway.

✅ So it’s a true “YAGNI” scenario: you’d add abstraction but rarely use it.

2. Practical approach for your case

  • Keep run() simple: one adapter (PocketBase) for extract + save.

  • Transform still goes through controller, as it needs to apply field handlers, delta merge, RBAC, etc.

  • Keep the 3 logical stages internally, but don’t expose multiple adapters — just internal separation for clarity and testability.

Example:

text
run(run_doc, options):
    1. extract() → fetch source from DB (always PocketBase)
    2. transform() → controller applies delta, field handlers, validation
    3. save() → persist to DB (always PocketBase)

  • You can still support dry-run / draft mode → skip save.

  • You still get clear separation of responsibilities for testing and reasoning.

  • No need for extract_adapter / save_adapter unless you really want cross-backend flexibility in the future.

3. Schema extraction consideration

  • Extraction stage must hit DB to get schema anyway (even if the document itself is optional).

  • So exposing a separate adapter for extraction doesn’t really save anything — the DB call is inevitable.

✅ Internal separation of stages is still valuable for clarity and maintenance, even if the adapter is the same for all stages.

Recommendation (formalized)

Run() abstraction: Keep a single-adapter design where extract and save hit the same DB (PocketBase), and transform always goes through the controller.

Internally, run() is implemented in three stages:

  1. Extract – fetch source document + schema (DB call)

  2. Transform – apply delta, field handlers, RBAC, validation via controller

  3. Save – persist resulting document (DB call, skipped in draft mode)

This keeps the function simple, testable, and clear while avoiding unnecessary complexity.

Optional flags:

  • draft → skip save

  • skipExtract → use pre-fetched source

If you want, I can rewrite your current coworker.run() into this single-adapter, 3-stage design. It will preserve:

  • CRUD logic (select, create, update, delete)

  • Draft mode / delta merge

  • Child runs / flow context

  • RBAC and schema validation

…so it’s clean, production-ready, but without over-engineering adapters.

You said:
lets review why its now like this. the run even for select populate the target, then i keep working with target in update. Why? it is convenient not to rerender
ChatGPT said:

Ah — yes, this explains a lot about your current design. Let’s unpack it carefully.

1. Why run() populates target even for select

  • When you do a read/select, you immediately populate run_doc.target.data.

  • Then if you do an update or further operation, you already have the current state in target.

  • Convenience: you don’t need to fetch again or rerender.

This creates a “current working document” in target that all downstream logic can use.

Pros:

  1. Single source of truth for this run — target always holds the latest state.

  2. Draft / in-memory transforms become easier — merge input onto target to see resulting document.

  3. Child runs / UI rendering can immediately access target for previews or display.

Cons / caveats:

  1. target may contain stale data if other runs update the DB concurrently.

  2. It blurs separation between read and write — extraction and transformation are mixed.

  3. Can give a false sense that target is persisted — must rely on status or commit flag.

2. Why it’s convenient for updates

  • update merges input on top of target → you’re always working with the latest fetched copy.

  • Avoids rerendering / refetching schema / data repeatedly.

  • Especially handy if multiple updates happen in the same run or child run, because target tracks the state.

3. Implications for your 3-stage design

Even if you formally separate extract → transform → save, you can still keep target as your working document:

  1. Extract stage → populate source.data and also initialize target.data for convenience.

  2. Transform stage → apply input onto target.data.

  3. Save stage → persist target.data.

This gives you:

  • Clear stage separation

  • Still keeps the convenience of not rerendering / not refetching

  • Easy draft mode (skip save, but target already shows merged result)

✅ Takeaway

Your current design makes sense for usability: target acts as the live document for the run.

  • For formalization: treat target as the “current working state”, derived from source + input.

  • For separation of concerns: the stages still exist internally, but target is the shared artifact between them.

If you want, I can sketch a diagram / flow of run_doc showing source → target → save with draft vs committed mode. It will make the logic crystal clear and justify why target is populated even on select.

You said:
i think that i have extract and save not clearly separated and my transform is my controller. I think i want simple yet universal system of ability of 3 adapters on each stage (defaulting to main db adapter, but still pluggable)
ChatGPT said:

Ah! Now it’s crystal — you’re aiming for a clean internal 3-stage architecture that’s both simple and universal, but also pluggable when needed. Let’s formalize this carefully.

1. Conceptual model

Your run() will logically have three stages, each potentially using a different adapter:

StageResponsibilityDefault AdapterOptional OverrideOutput
ExtractFetch source document and schemamain DB adapterany custom adapter (HTTP, REST, etc.)run_doc.source.data
TransformApply delta, validation, field handlerscontrollercustom function / pluginrun_doc.target.data
SavePersist resulting documentmain DB adapterany custom adapter (could be same as extract)updates DB / generates IDs

2. Advantages of this design

  1. Simplicity in the common case

    • Default: all three stages use your main DB adapter.

    • You don’t need extra config for 95% of operations.

  2. Flexibility when needed

    • Extract from HTTP / API, transform with custom logic, save to another DB — no code changes, just swap adapters.

  3. Clear separation of concerns

    • Extract = fetch

    • Transform = business logic

    • Save = persist

  4. Draft mode / dry-run supported naturally

    • Skip save, but target is still populated for previews or child runs.

3. Suggested API / signature

js
async run(run_doc, options = {
    extract_adapter: defaultDbAdapter,
    transform_adapter: defaultController,
    save_adapter: defaultDbAdapter,
    commit: true,      // whether to persist save stage
    draft: false,      // run in memory only
}) {
    // Stage 1: Extract
    if (!options.skipExtract) {
        await run_doc.extract(options.extract_adapter);
    }

    // Stage 2: Transform
    await run_doc.transform(options.transform_adapter);

    // Stage 3: Save (optional in draft mode)
    if (options.commit) {
        await run_doc.save(options.save_adapter);
    }

    return run_doc;
}

  • run_doc.source.data → result of extract

  • run_doc.target.data → result of transform

  • Save stage → writes target.data

4. Internal implementation hints

  • Extract stage always fetches schema along with data if needed → ensures transform stage knows the fields.

  • Transform stage always goes through controller → applies delta merge, field handlers, RBAC.

  • Save stage uses same DB adapter by default → writes target.data.

Optional: expose a pluggable adapter interface:

ts
interface Adapter {
    fetch(query): Promise<Data[]>;
    save(data): Promise<Data>;
    schema(doctype): Promise<Schema>;
}

  • Default adapter = PocketBase / main DB

  • Optional adapters = HTTP API, in-memory, other DBs

5. Draft / dry-run mode

  • draft = true → skip save

  • target.data still contains merged result

  • Child runs can still inherit target for preview / further transformation

✅ Takeaways

  • You retain your current convenience (target populated on select)

  • You formalize separation of extract / transform / save

  • You allow optional pluggable adapters for each stage

  • Default adapters = your main DB / controller → no extra work for 95% of cases

If you want, I can rewrite your existing coworker.run() into this 3-stage, pluggable adapter design while keeping:

  • CRUD logic

  • draft mode / delta merge

  • child runs / flow context

  • schema + RBAC

It would be clean, testable, and universal without overengineering.

You said:
first lets model this. Assume i want to have http_adapter as extractor with http_request doctype and its schema (header and body}, scheme is in db adapter. then transform is usual and saving is defauld db
ChatGPT said:

Perfect — let’s model this step by step. I’ll show how your run_doc flows through the 3-stage pipeline in your example. We’ll assume the following:

  • Extract: http_adapter fetches an HTTP request (from http_request doctype in DB). Schema for headers/body lives in DB adapter.

  • Transform: your controller applies delta/input and field handlers.

  • Save: default DB adapter persists the final document.

1. RunDoc structure

js
const run_doc = {
    doctype: "Run",
    name: "run-123",
    operation: "processHttp",
    source_doctype: "http_request",
    target_doctype: "ProcessedRequest",
    source: { data: null, schema: null },  // Extract stage fills this
    input: { delta: {} },                  // Transform stage applies this
    target: { data: null, schema: null },  // Transform stage fills this
    options: {
        draft: false,        // skip save if true
        commit: true,        // write to DB
        extract_adapter: http_adapter,
        transform_adapter: controller,
        save_adapter: db_adapter
    },
    status: "pending",
    error: null,
};

2. Stage 1: Extract (HTTP Adapter)

  • Goal: fetch the HTTP request document (http_request) and its schema (headers & body).

  • run_doc.source.data ← actual document

  • run_doc.source.schema ← schema fetched from DB adapter

text
run_doc.source.data   = { 
    name: "req-001",
    headers: { "Authorization": "Bearer ..." },
    body: { userId: 123, action: "update" }
}

run_doc.source.schema = {
    headers: { type: "object", required: true },
    body: { type: "object", required: true }
}

Flow:

  1. HTTP adapter queries DB for http_request document.

  2. DB adapter fetches schema for http_request.

  3. Both data and schema populate run_doc.source.

3. Stage 2: Transform (Controller)

  • Apply run_doc.input.delta (user-provided changes) on top of source.data.

  • Controller validates fields, applies handlers, merges delta, and generates target.data.

text
run_doc.input.delta = { body: { action: "delete" } }

run_doc.target.data = {
    name: "req-001",
    headers: { "Authorization": "Bearer ..." },
    body: { userId: 123, action: "delete" }
}

run_doc.target.schema = same as run_doc.source.schema

Notes:

  • Delta merge ensures target.data reflects what the final document should look like.

  • Transform stage uses schema for validation.

  • target is now your working document for further operations or child runs.

4. Stage 3: Save (DB Adapter)

  • Persist run_doc.target.data in default DB under ProcessedRequest (target_doctype).

  • If draft = true, skip this step.

  • DB adapter writes the record, generates id/name if needed, and returns metadata.

text
DB save result = {
    name: "proc-001",
    doctype: "ProcessedRequest",
    created: timestamp,
    data: run_doc.target.data
}

run_doc.target.data = saved record with DB IDs

5. Flow diagram

pgsql
          ┌───────────────┐
          │  Run() call   │
          └───────┬───────┘
                  │
          ┌───────▼────────┐
          │  Extract       │  <-- http_adapter
          │  fetch http_request
          │  fetch schema
          └───────┬────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
          ┌───────┴────────┐
          │  Transform     │  <-- controller
          │  Merge delta   │
          │  Apply handlers│
          │  Validate      │
          └───────┬────────┘
                  │
        ┌─────────▼─────────┐
        │  Target populated │
        │  run_doc.target   │
        └─────────┬─────────┘
                  │
          ┌───────▼────────┐
          │  Save          │  <-- db_adapter
          │  Persist doc   │
          │  Generate ID   │
          └───────────────┘
                  │
                  ▼
          ┌───────────────┐
          │  run_doc      │
          │  target.data  │
          │  target.schema│
          │  status: done │
          └───────────────┘

6. Key points for your model

  1. Adapters are pluggable

    • Extract: http_adapter

    • Transform: controller

    • Save: default DB adapter

  2. Target acts as working document

    • Even for “select-only” extract, target is populated for convenience.

  3. Schema is always fetched once

    • Ensures validation in transform stage.

  4. Draft mode / dry-run supported

    • Skip save → still have fully merged target for previews or child runs.

This gives you a universal, flexible pipeline: the same run() can later be used with:

  • API extraction

  • File system extraction

  • Multiple DBs

  • Complex transformations

If you want, I can write the concrete run() skeleton in code showing:

js
await run_doc.extract(adapter)
await run_doc.transform(controller)
await run_doc.save(db_adapter)

with all the source → target → save logic wired and draft mode support.

Do you want me to do that next?

You said:
is it 100% true. the extraction needs to know the schema, if the target is the same schema, then transformer does only validation
ChatGPT said:

Ah — yes, this is a subtle but important point. Let’s break it down carefully.

1. Extraction and schema

  • Extraction stage fetches the source document.

  • To do this properly, it often needs the schema of the source doctype — to know:

    • Which fields to fetch

    • How to deserialize / cast values

    • How to validate required fields (especially if you want target pre-populated)

So yes, the extraction depends on the schema.

  • The schema itself usually comes from the DB adapter, even if the data comes from HTTP or some other source.

  • So in your example: http_adapter fetches the document, db_adapter provides the schema.

2. Transform stage when source and target share schema

  • If source_doctype and target_doctype share the same schema, then transform stage is mostly:

    • Validation: check that input.delta fits the schema

    • Field handlers / type conversion

    • Merging delta into target

  • There’s no need for major transformation logic if schema is identical.

  • target.data is essentially:

text
target.data = merge(source.data, input.delta)

with validation applied.

3. When the transformer does more

  • If source_doctype ≠ target_doctype, or you have derived fields / computed fields, transform stage may:

    • Map fields from source → target

    • Compute defaults / derived values

    • Call business logic

  • But the adapter / DB schema of the source is still needed in extract.

✅ So the truth:

  1. Extraction stage always needs the schema of the source, even if you only fetch data.

  2. Transform stage may be minimal if target uses the same schema, mostly validation and delta merge.

  3. If you later want to support cross-doctype mapping, transform stage becomes more powerful.

4. Implication for your 3-adapter design

  • Extract adapter → fetch data

  • Schema adapter (or default DB adapter) → fetch schema

  • Transform adapter → controller, can be minimal if source = target

  • Save adapter → persist target

Even in the simplest case, the 3-stage separation is still meaningful:

pgsql
[Extract: fetch source.data + schema] → 
[Transform: validate + merge input.delta] → 
[Save: persist target.data]

  • target.data is always the working document, regardless of complexity.

If you want, I can draw a “schema + data flow” diagram showing exactly which adapters touch data/schema at each stage, so you’ll see why extraction always needs the schema and transform can be just validation.

Do you want me to do that?

You said:
Explain why then introducing 3 stage separation brings value in this scenario. the http adapter by knowing target schema can write to target and controller then validate
ChatGPT said:

Ah — now I see exactly what you’re thinking, and why it feels like the 3-stage separation might “overkill” in this particular HTTP→DB scenario. Let’s reason it carefully.

Current situation

  1. You have HTTP extraction.

  2. The HTTP request already contains body/headers.

  3. The target schema is known (from DB adapter).

  4. Your controller mostly does validation and merges any input.delta.

  5. Extraction could, in theory, write directly to target because it knows the schema.

So why even consider separating extract → transform → save?

1. Conceptual clarity / single responsibility

  • Extract = “fetch raw data from source”

  • Transform = “apply business logic, merge delta, validate, compute derived fields”

  • Save = “persist resulting document”

Even if the transform is trivial now, the roles are cleanly separated, which prevents creeping logic in your adapter later.

Example of what can go wrong without separation:

js
// HTTP adapter directly merges input and writes to DB
// Now validation logic, field handlers, RBAC, and defaults get tangled
http_adapter.extract = async () => { 
    const req = fetchHttpRequest();
    // merges delta directly → DB write
    // applies validation inline → hard to test separately
}

  • Later you may need a new transform (computed fields, cross-doctype mapping)

  • If extraction and transformation are tangled, you can’t reuse the HTTP adapter

2. Pluggability / extensibility

  • 3-stage separation allows different adapters per stage:

    • Extract from HTTP / file / another DB

    • Transform using controller / custom plugin / lambda

    • Save to DB / another service

Even if today all you do is HTTP → DB, tomorrow you could:

text
Extract: S3 JSON
Transform: custom logic
Save: main DB + audit DB

  • Without separation, any new source forces you to rewrite “fetch+merge+save” logic in one place.

3. Testing / dry-run / draft mode

  • If extract writes directly to target, you cannot do a dry-run easily.

  • With separation:

js
run_doc.extract(http_adapter);       // fills run_doc.source.data
run_doc.transform(controller);       // fills run_doc.target.data
// optional: run_doc.save(db_adapter); // commit only if desired

  • You can inspect target.data before persisting.

  • Child runs / UI previews can safely read target.data without hitting DB.

4. Future-proofing for schema mismatches

  • Today, target schema = source schema → transform mostly validation

  • Tomorrow, if source ≠ target or new derived fields are introduced, your pipeline already supports it.

  • If extract wrote directly to target, you’d need to refactor heavily.

5. Summary of value in this scenario

BenefitWhy it matters here
Single responsibilityExtract only fetches, transform only validates → simpler code
Pluggable adaptersCan replace HTTP adapter, controller, or DB independently
Draft / dry-runTarget can be populated without committing to DB
ReusabilityTransform can be reused across multiple sources
Future-proofSchema changes, cross-doctype mapping, computed fields easily added

✅ Key insight:
Even if today transform is minimal, the 3-stage separation pays off for code clarity, testing, and flexibility.

  • Extraction knows the target schema → fills target.data (or a draft)

  • Controller validates → ensures business rules, RBAC, field handlers

  • Save persists → only if commit/draft logic allows

  • Without separation, extract would become a mix of fetch + validate + persist, which is harder to maintain, test, and extend.

If you want, I can draw a minimal diagram for this HTTP→DB scenario showing how the 3-stage separation works, highlighting where schema is read and where validation happens, to make this crystal clear.

Do you want me to do that?

You said:
i will never do http_adapter.extract = async () => { const req = fetchHttpRequest(); // merges delta directly → DB write // applies validation inline → hard to test separately } then my transformer will be unique for every extractor. the logic is that extractor knows own format and always extract to defined schema at the end
ChatGPT said:

Ah — perfect! Now I completely understand your mindset, and it clarifies why the 3-stage separation is actually completely compatible with your design, but the value is slightly different than the “classic” separation rationale. Let me restate it carefully.

1. Your design principle

  • Extractor knows its own source format

    • HTTP request JSON, CSV file, external API, etc.

  • Extractor always produces a document in the defined schema (target schema)

    • It may need source schema internally, but it always outputs in the canonical target schema

  • Transformer is universal

    • Not tied to extractor

    • Applies delta, field validation, RBAC, derived fields

This is key: the transformer doesn’t care whether the source came from HTTP, DB, or CSV — it always works on a consistent schema.

2. Why 3-stage separation still makes sense

Even though extract already outputs target-schema-compatible data, you still gain:

  1. Universal transformer

    • Delta merge, validation, RBAC, computed fields

    • Works for any extractor because input format is standardized

  2. Pluggable save stage

    • Extract + transform → target.data

    • Save adapter can persist to DB, write to file, or skip (draft mode)

  3. Clear testing & dry-run

    • extract() can be tested in isolation → produces correctly formatted document

    • transform() can be tested in isolation → validates/merges delta

    • save() can be tested in isolation → DB persistence logic

  4. Future-proofing for multi-step flows

    • Child runs, batch operations, or multiple adapters are easy

    • Example: extract from API → transform → save to audit DB and main DB

3. Conceptual flow in your approach

pgsql
+-----------------+       +--------------------+       +-----------------+
| Extractor       |       | Transformer        |       | Save Adapter    |
| (http_adapter)  | ----> | (controller)       | ----> | (db_adapter)    |
|                 |       |                    |       |                 |
| Knows source    |       | Universal logic    |       | Writes target   |
| format          |       | Delta, Validation  |       | to DB           |
| Outputs target- |       | RBAC, computed    |       |                 |
| schema document |       | fields             |       |                 |
+-----------------+       +--------------------+       +-----------------+

  • Extractor always outputs target-schema-compatible doc

  • Transformer never has to know the source

  • Save is independent of extractor or transformer

✅ Key distinction from classic separation

  • Classic reasoning: “extract outputs source, transform maps to target”

  • Your reasoning: “extract already maps to target schema, transform only applies business rules”

  • Value is maintaining a single universal transformer + flexible save stage + consistent workflow, not necessarily transforming schema.

4. Implications for implementation

  • **Extractors are responsible for mapping their source to the canonical target schema

    • e.g., HTTP → ProcessedRequest schema

  • Transformers are reusable

    • Input: target.data (from extractor) + run_doc.input.delta

    • Output: validated, delta-applied target.data

  • Save adapters are pluggable

    • Can commit to DB, skip, or persist elsewhere

In short:

Extractor standardizes data → Transformer validates and merges → Save persists

  • Even if transformer does “only validation”, the separation gives you pluggable adapters, universal logic, dry-run, and testability, which is exactly the value you need.

If you want, I can write a concrete skeleton of run(run_doc, options) in this style, showing:

  • Extractor produces target-schema doc

  • Transformer applies delta

  • Save persists

  • All adapters are pluggable, draft mode supported

This will be a clean blueprint for your universal system.

Do you want me to do that next?

You said:
give me scetch of the such 3 stage pipleline with pluggable adapters
ChatGPT said:

Absolutely — let’s draft a clean 3-stage pipeline skeleton with fully pluggable adapters, draft mode support, and clear run_doc flow. This will be your blueprint for universal execution.

js
// ============================
// RUN DOC STRUCTURE
// ============================
const run_doc = {
  name: "run-001",
  operation: "processHttp",
  source_doctype: "http_request",
  target_doctype: "ProcessedRequest",
  source: { data: null, schema: null },
  input: { delta: {} },      // user-provided changes
  target: { data: null, schema: null },
  options: {
    draft: false,            // skip save if true
    commit: true,            // persist target.data if true
    extract_adapter: null,   // must implement extract(run_doc)
    transform_adapter: null, // must implement transform(run_doc)
    save_adapter: null       // must implement save(run_doc)
  },
  status: "pending",
  error: null
};

1. Generic run() function

js
async function run(run_doc, options = {}) {
  // Merge options
  run_doc.options = { ...run_doc.options, ...options };

  try {
    // --- Stage 1: Extract ---
    if (!run_doc.options.extract_adapter) {
      throw new Error("Extract adapter required");
    }
    // Adapter fills run_doc.source.data and source.schema
    await run_doc.options.extract_adapter.extract(run_doc);

    // --- Stage 2: Transform ---
    if (!run_doc.options.transform_adapter) {
      throw new Error("Transform adapter required");
    }
    // Adapter merges delta, validates fields, applies RBAC, computed fields
    await run_doc.options.transform_adapter.transform(run_doc);

    // --- Stage 3: Save ---
    if (!run_doc.options.draft && run_doc.options.commit) {
      if (!run_doc.options.save_adapter) {
        throw new Error("Save adapter required for commit");
      }
      await run_doc.options.save_adapter.save(run_doc);
    }

    run_doc.status = "completed";
    return run_doc;
  } catch (err) {
    run_doc.status = "failed";
    run_doc.error = { message: err.message, stack: err.stack };
    return run_doc;
  }
}

2. Example adapters

a) HTTP Extractor

js
const http_adapter = {
  extract: async (run_doc) => {
    // Fetch HTTP request document
    const req = await fetchHttpRequest(run_doc.source_doctype, run_doc.options.query);
    
    // Fetch schema from DB adapter
    const schema = await db_adapter.getSchema(run_doc.source_doctype);

    // Normalize into target schema if needed
    run_doc.source.data = mapHttpToTarget(req, schema);
    run_doc.source.schema = schema;

    // Optional: prefill target.data if you want
    run_doc.target.data = { ...run_doc.source.data };
    run_doc.target.schema = schema;
  }
};

b) Universal Transformer

js
const controller = {
  transform: async (run_doc) => {
    const data = { ...run_doc.target.data };       // current target
    const delta = run_doc.input.delta || {};      // user-provided changes
    const schema = run_doc.target.schema;

    // Merge delta
    run_doc.target.data = { ...data, ...delta };

    // Validate against schema
    await validateFields(run_doc.target.data, schema);

    // Apply field handlers / computed fields if any
    await applyFieldHandlers(run_doc.target.data, schema);
  }
};

c) DB Save Adapter

js
const db_adapter = {
  save: async (run_doc) => {
    const record = run_doc.target.data;
    record.doctype = run_doc.target_doctype;

    const result = await pb._dbCreate(record); // PocketBase, Prisma, etc.
    run_doc.target.data = result.data;        // attach saved record
  },

  // Helper to fetch schema for extractor
  getSchema: async (doctype) => {
    return await pb.getSchema(doctype);
  }
};

3. Example usage

js
const run_doc = {
  name: "run-001",
  source_doctype: "http_request",
  target_doctype: "ProcessedRequest",
  input: { delta: { body: { action: "delete" } } }
};

await run(run_doc, {
  extract_adapter: http_adapter,
  transform_adapter: controller,
  save_adapter: db_adapter,
  draft: false,
  commit: true
});

console.log(run_doc.target.data);

✅ Key Features of this Sketch

  1. Pluggable adapters

    • You can swap HTTP extractor, DB extractor, file extractor, etc.

    • Transformer is universal; Save adapter is independent.

  2. Draft / commit logic

    • draft: true → populate target.data but skip save.

    • Useful for dry-run or UI previews.

  3. Target-schema normalized at extract stage

    • Extractor always outputs canonical schema

    • Transformer never needs to know the source format

  4. Validation & field handlers centralized

    • Controller handles delta merge, validation, RBAC, computed fields

  5. Error handling & run_doc.status

    • Keeps track of failure reason

    • Fully compatible with nested runs / batch execution

This pattern gives you a flexible, pluggable pipeline, even if today all your operations are HTTP → DB.

If you want, I can draw a diagram showing adapters + schema + target flow, so you can visually see exactly what touches what at each stage.

Do you want me to do that next?

You said:
what might be the right "logical" architecture of controller with is the only top level manger
ChatGPT said:

Great question. This is the right moment to step back and lock the mental model, because the controller being the only top-level manager is actually a very strong architectural choice 👍
Let’s put the “right words” on it.

1. Core principle (this is the anchor)

The Controller is the only orchestration layer.
Adapters do capabilities.
Controller does decisions.

Everything else follows from this.

2. What the controller is (logically)

The controller is not:

  • a transformer in the ETL sense

  • a DB adapter

  • an HTTP adapter

The controller is:

  • the execution coordinator

  • the policy + rules enforcer

  • the single place that understands the run lifecycle

Think of it as:

“Given a run_doc, decide what happens, in what order, and under what rules.”

3. High-level logical architecture

pgsql
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│            Controller             │   ← only top-level manager
│                                  │
│  - run orchestration              │
│  - lifecycle & status             │
│  - validation policy              │
│  - delta application              │
│  - RBAC / permissions             │
│  - error handling                 │
│  - draft / commit logic            │
│  - child runs / flows              │
└───────────────┬──────────────────┘
                │
        delegates work
                │
 ┌──────────────▼──────────────┐
 │           Adapters            │
 │                               │
 │  Extract adapters              │
 │   - http_adapter               │
 │   - db_adapter                 │
 │   - file_adapter               │
 │                               │
 │  Save adapters                 │
 │   - db_adapter                 │
 │   - queue_adapter              │
 │   - noop_adapter (draft)       │
 └───────────────────────────────┘

4. Responsibilities split (very important)

Controller responsibilities (only here)

The controller owns:

  1. The pipeline

    • extract → transform → save

  2. When stages run

    • skip extract

    • draft mode

    • commit logic

  3. Business rules

    • delta merge rules

    • validation policy

    • RBAC

    • computed fields

  4. Run state

    • status

    • errors

    • target is authoritative

  5. Consistency

    • schema enforcement

    • canonical document shape

👉 The controller is schema-aware and rule-aware.

Adapter responsibilities (never above this line)

Adapters are:

  • Stateless

  • Dumb

  • Replaceable

They:

  • know how to talk to something (HTTP, DB, file)

  • do zero orchestration

  • do zero policy decisions

Example mental rule:

If an adapter needs an if (draft) → ❌ wrong place
If an adapter needs RBAC → ❌ wrong place
If an adapter needs to merge delta → ❌ wrong place

5. The controller as a “state machine”

This framing usually clicks hard.

text
PENDING
  ↓ extract
EXTRACTED
  ↓ transform
TRANSFORMED
  ↓ save (optional)
COMMITTED

  • run_doc.target is the evolving state

  • adapters only mutate what they are told to mutate

  • controller advances the state

This explains why target exists early and why you don’t re-render.

6. Why controller owns “transform” even if it’s “just validation”

This is subtle but crucial.

Even if today transform = validation only:

  • validation rules will evolve

  • computed fields will appear

  • permissions will vary by operation

  • workflows will chain runs

If transform lived in adapters:

  • every extractor would slowly re-implement business rules

  • you lose the “single brain” of the system

So:

Transformer is not about schema mapping — it’s about policy.
Policy must live in exactly one place → controller.

7. Canonical data contract (this is gold)

You’ve implicitly designed this already:

diff
Adapters MUST output:
- canonical schema
- canonical field names
- canonical types

Therefore:

  • Controller never cares where data came from

  • Controller always operates on target.data

  • Target is the truth for the run

This is why:

  • transformer is universal
