  • extractor-specific logic stays contained

  • system remains extensible

8. Minimal but correct controller API

This is a clean mental model:

ts
controller.run(run_doc, {
  extract_adapter,
  save_adapter,
  draft,
  commit
})

Internally:

ts
controller.run = async (run_doc) => {
  await controller.extract(run_doc)
  await controller.transform(run_doc)
  await controller.save(run_doc)
}

  • Extract/save are delegations

  • Transform is owned

9. One-sentence architecture definition (you can reuse this)

The controller is the single orchestration and policy layer that manages the run lifecycle, while adapters provide pluggable IO capabilities and always output canonical, schema-compliant data.

If you put this sentence at the top of your repo, future-you will thank you.

10. Reality check: your intuition is correct

You were right that:

  • extractor can normalize to target schema

  • transformer does not need to be per-extractor

  • re-render avoidance is a valid design goal

The missing piece was not what you’re doing, but naming the controller as the only manager and framing adapters as pure capabilities.

You’re designing a workflow engine, not a CRUD wrapper.

If you want, next we can:

  • formalize adapter interfaces (extract/save)

  • define invariants for run_doc

  • model child runs & flows cleanly

  • or pressure-test this architecture with a “bad future requirement” and see if it breaks

Just tell me where you want to stress it 💥

You said:
assume 2 things. All data is in frappe like doctype even config, etc. All states, transitions and rules on document level and above (other then FieldSystem as FiSy operates on field level) is defined in state machines (FSMs). FSM is json document of the same structure. FSM Sequencer, universal (that plays the sequences) should be separate part of controller. { "doctype": "State Machine", "name": "Document_FSM", "statemachine_name": "Document_FSM", "core_dimensions": ["is_submittable", "docstatus", "autosave_enabled", "dirty"], "core_states": { "is_submittable": { "values": [0, 1], "options": ["false", "true"] }, "docstatus": { "values": [0, 1, 2], "options": ["Draft", "Submitted", "Cancelled"] }, "autosave_enabled": { "values": [0, 1], "options": ["false", "true"] }, "dirty": { "values": [0, 1], "options": ["clean", "dirty"] } }, "default_dimensions": ["save", "submit", "validate", "cancel"], "default_states": { "save": { "values": [0, 1, 2, -1], "options": ["idle", "pending", "success", "error"], "transitions": { "0": [1], "1": [2, -1], "2": [0], "-1": [0, 1] } }, "submit": { "values": [0, 1, 2, -1], "options": ["idle", "pending", "success", "error"], "transitions": { "0": [1], "1": [2, -1], "2": [0], "-1": [0, 1] } }, "validate": { "values": [0, 1, 2, -1], "options": ["idle", "pending", "valid", "invalid"], "transitions": { "0": [1], "1": [2, -1], "2": [0], "-1": [0, 1] } }, "cancel": { "values": [0, 1, 2, -1], "options": ["idle", "pending", "success", "error"], "transitions": { "0": [1], "1": [2, -1], "2": [0], "-1": [0, 1] } } }, "workflow_dimensions": { "Sales Order": ["approve", "ship"], "Purchase Order": ["approve", "receive"], "Task": [] }, "workflow_states": { "approve": { "values": [0, 1, 2, -1], "options": ["idle", "pending", "approved", "rejected"], "transitions": { "0": [1], "1": [2, -1], "2": [0], "-1": [0, 1] } }, "ship": { "values": [0, 1, 2, -1], "options": ["idle", "pending", "shipped", "error"], "transitions": { "0": [1], "1": [2, -1], "2": [0], "-1": [0, 1] } }, "receive": { "values": [0, 1, 2, -1], "options": ["idle", "pending", "received", "error"], "transitions": { "0": [1], "1": [2, -1], "2": [0], "-1": [0, 1] } } }, "rules": { "save": { "0_to_1": { "requires": { "docstatus": 0, "dirty": 1, "validate": 2, "submit": 0, "cancel": 0 } }, "-1_to_1": { "requires": { "docstatus": 0, "dirty": 1, "validate": 2, "submit": 0, "cancel": 0 } } }, "submit": { "0_to_1": { "requires": { "is_submittable": 1, "docstatus": 0, "dirty": 0, "validate": 2, "save": 0, "cancel": 0 } }, "-1_to_1": { "requires": { "is_submittable": 1, "docstatus": 0, "dirty": 0, "validate": 2, "save": 0, "cancel": 0 } } }, "validate": { "0_to_1": { "requires": { "save": 0, "submit": 0, "cancel": 0 } }, "-1_to_1": { "requires": { "save": 0, "submit": 0, "cancel": 0 } } }, "cancel": { "0_to_1": { "requires": { "is_submittable": 1, "docstatus": 1, "save": 0, "submit": 0 } }, "-1_to_1": { "requires": { "is_submittable": 1, "docstatus": 1, "save": 0, "submit": 0 } } } }, "sequences": { "save": { "steps": [ { "dimension": 0, "transition": 1 }, { "execute": "validate", "onSuccess": { "dimension": 2, "transition": 2 }, "onFailure": { "dimension": 2, "transition": -1, "then": [ { "dimension": 0, "transition": -1 }, { "stop": true } ] } }, { "execute": "dbSave", "onSuccess": { "dimension": 0, "transition": 2, "then": [ { "core_dimension": 3, "value": 0 } ] }, "onFailure": { "dimension": 0, "transition": -1, "stop": true } }, { "dimension": 0, "transition": 0 } ] }, "submit": { "steps": [ { "dimension": 1, "transition": 1 }, { "execute": "validate", "onSuccess": { "dimension": 2, "transition": 2 }, "onFailure": { "dimension": 2, "transition": -1, "then": [ { "dimension": 1, "transition": -1 }, { "stop": true } ] } }, { "execute": "dbSubmit", "onSuccess": { "dimension": 1, "transition": 2, "then": [ { "core_dimension": 1, "value": 1 } ] }, "onFailure": { "dimension": 1, "transition": -1, "stop": true } }, { "dimension": 1, "transition": 0 } ] }, "cancel": { "steps": [ { "dimension": 3, "transition": 1 }, { "execute": "dbCancel", "onSuccess": { "dimension": 3, "transition": 2, "then": [ { "core_dimension": 1, "value": 2 } ] }, "onFailure": { "dimension": 3, "transition": -1, "stop": true } }, { "dimension": 3, "transition": 0 } ] }, "validate": { "steps": [ { "dimension": 2, "transition": 1 }, { "execute": "validateDocument", "onSuccess": { "dimension": 2, "transition": 2 }, "onFailure": { "dimension": 2, "transition": -1, "stop": true } }, { "dimension": 2, "transition": 0 } ] } } } // ====STATE MACHINE EVALUATOR==== // ============================================================================ // HELPER FUNCTIONS // ============================================================================ function evaluateFSM(fsmConfig, vector_state, doctype) { const available = []; // Get dimensions based on doctype const dimensions = [ ...fsmConfig.default_dimensions, ...(fsmConfig.workflow_dimensions[doctype] || []) ]; // Early exit: If docstatus != 0, check only cancel if (vector_state.docstatus !== 0) { if (vector_state.docstatus === 1 && dimensions.includes('cancel')) { const cancelIndex = dimensions.indexOf('cancel'); const stateConfig = fsmConfig.default_states.cancel; return evaluateDimension( cancelIndex, 'cancel', stateConfig, fsmConfig.rules.cancel, vector_state ); } return []; } // Evaluate all dimensions for (let i = 0; i < dimensions.length; i++) { const dimensionName = dimensions[i]; // Get state config from default or workflow const stateConfig = fsmConfig.default_states[dimensionName] || fsmConfig.workflow_states[dimensionName]; if (!stateConfig) continue; const transitions = evaluateDimension( i, dimensionName, stateConfig, fsmConfig.rules[dimensionName], vector_state ); available.push(...transitions); } return available; } function evaluateDimension(dimensionIndex, dimensionName, stateConfig, rules, vector_state) { const available = []; const currentValue = vector_state.default[dimensionIndex]; // Get possible next values from transitions const possibleNext = stateConfig.transitions[String(currentValue)] || []; if (possibleNext.length === 0) return []; // Check each possible transition for (const nextValue of possibleNext) { const ruleKey = ${currentValue}_to_${nextValue}; const rule = rules?.[ruleKey]; // No rule = transition always allowed if (!rule) { available.push({ dimensionIndex, dimensionName, from: currentValue, to: nextValue, action: nextValue === 1 ? dimensionName : null }); continue; } // Check requirements if (checkRequirements(rule.requires, vector_state)) { available.push({ dimensionIndex, dimensionName, from: currentValue, to: nextValue, action: nextValue === 1 ? dimensionName : null }); } } return available; } function checkRequirements(requires, vector_state) { if (!requires) return true; // Check each requirement for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(requires)) { if (!checkSingleRequirement(key, value, vector_state)) { return false; } } return true; } function checkSingleRequirement(key, value, vector_state) { // Check core state first if (vector_state.core && key in vector_state.core) { const actualValue = vector_state.core[key]; return Array.isArray(value) ? value.includes(actualValue) : actualValue === value; } // Check default dimensions if (vector_state.default && key in vector_state.default) { const actualValue = vector_state.default[key]; return Array.isArray(value) ? value.includes(actualValue) : actualValue === value; } // Check workflow dimensions if (vector_state.workflow && key in vector_state.workflow) { const actualValue = vector_state.workflow[key]; return Array.isArray(value) ? value.includes(actualValue) : actualValue === value; } return false; } // ============================================================================ // COWORKER.FSM MODULE // ============================================================================ coworker.FSM = { config: null, async load() { const result = await coworker.run({ operation: "takeone", from: "State Machine", query: { where: { statemachine_name: "Document_FSM" } } }); if (!result.success || !result.target.data[0]) { throw new Error("State Machine not found"); } this.config = result.target.data[0]; console.log("✅ FSM loaded:", this.config.statemachine_name); }, // Convert document state to flat vector_state for evaluation buildVectorState(doc, doctype) { const fsm = this.config; const vectorState = {}; // Map core dimensions fsm.core_dimensions.forEach((dim, i) => { vectorState[dim] = doc.fsm_state.core[i]; }); // Map default dimensions fsm.default_dimensions.forEach((dim, i) => { vectorState[dim] = doc.fsm_state.default[i]; }); // Map workflow dimensions const workflowDims = fsm.workflow_dimensions[doctype] || []; workflowDims.forEach((dim, i) => { vectorState[dim] = doc.fsm_state.workflow[i]; }); // Keep original structure for requirements checking vectorState.core = {}; vectorState.default = {}; vectorState.workflow = {}; fsm.core_dimensions.forEach((dim, i) => { vectorState.core[dim] = doc.fsm_state.core[i]; }); fsm.default_dimensions.forEach((dim, i) => { vectorState.default[dim] = doc.fsm_state.default[i]; }); workflowDims.forEach((dim, i) => { vectorState.workflow[dim] = doc.fsm_state.workflow[i]; }); return vectorState; }, evaluate(doc, doctype) { if (!this.config) { throw new Error("FSM not loaded. Call FSM.load() first."); } const vectorState = this.buildVectorState(doc, doctype); return evaluateFSM(this.config, vectorState, doctype); }, getActionsForState(doc, doctype) { return this.evaluate(doc, doctype) .map(t => t.action) .filter(Boolean); }, canExecuteAction(action, doc, doctype) { return this.getActionsForState(doc, doctype).includes(action); }, // Initialize FSM state for new document initializeState(doctype, schema) { const fsm = this.config; // Initialize core const core = fsm.core_dimensions.map(dim => { if (dim === 'is_submittable') return schema.is_submittable ? 1 : 0; if (dim === 'docstatus') return 0; if (dim === 'autosave_enabled') return 0; if (dim === 'dirty') return 0; return 0; }); // Initialize default (all idle = 0) const defaultVector = fsm.default_dimensions.map(() => 0); // Initialize workflow (all idle = 0) const workflowDims = fsm.workflow_dimensions[doctype] || []; const workflowVector = workflowDims.map(() => 0); return { core, default: defaultVector, workflow: workflowVector }; } }; console.log("✅ FSM module loaded"); //NOT IMPLEMENTED //Exactly! You Need a Sequence Player //You already have the music sheet (FSM sequences), you just need the orchestra conductor (sequence player) to execute it. //javascript// ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // SEQUENCE PLAYER - The Missing Piece // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ coworker.FSM.playSequence = async function(actionName, doc, doctype) { const sequence = this.config.sequences[actionName]; if (!sequence) { throw new Error(No sequence defined for action: ${actionName}); } console.log(🎬 Playing sequence: ${actionName}); // Execute each step in order for (let i = 0; i < sequence.steps.length; i++) { const step = sequence.steps[i]; console.log( Step ${i + 1}/${sequence.steps.length}:, step); try { await this._executeStep(step, doc, doctype); } catch (error) { console.error(❌ Step ${i + 1} failed:, error); throw error; } } console.log(✅ Sequence complete: ${actionName}); return doc; }; // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // STEP EXECUTOR - Interprets Each Step Type // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ coworker.FSM._executeStep = async function(step, doc, doctype) { // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 1: Update FSM dimension state // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.dimension !== undefined && step.transition !== undefined) { console.log( → Update dimension ${step.dimension} to ${step.transition}); doc.fsm_state.default[step.dimension] = step.transition; return; } // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 2: Update core dimension // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.core_dimension !== undefined && step.value !== undefined) { console.log( → Update core[${step.core_dimension}] to ${step.value}); doc.fsm_state.core[step.core_dimension] = step.value; return; } // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 3: Execute sub-operation // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.execute) { console.log( → Execute: ${step.execute}); try { await this._executeOperation(step.execute, doc, doctype); // Handle onSuccess if (step.onSuccess) { console.log( ✅ Success, executing onSuccess); await this._handleOutcome(step.onSuccess, doc, doctype); } } catch (error) { console.log( ❌ Failed, executing onFailure); // Handle onFailure if (step.onFailure) { await this._handleOutcome(step.onFailure, doc, doctype); if (step.onFailure.stop) { throw new Error(Sequence stopped: ${error.message}); } } else { throw error; } } return; } // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 4: Stop execution // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.stop) { console.log( 🛑 Stop sequence); throw new Error('Sequence stopped by step directive'); } }; // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // OUTCOME HANDLER - Process onSuccess/onFailure // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ coworker.FSM._handleOutcome = async function(outcome, doc, doctype) { // Update dimension if specified if (outcome.dimension !== undefined && outcome.transition !== undefined) { console.log( → Set dimension ${outcome.dimension} = ${outcome.transition}); doc.fsm_state.default[outcome.dimension] = outcome.transition; } // Update core dimension if specified if (outcome.core_dimension !== undefined && outcome.value !== undefined) { console.log( → Set core[${outcome.core_dimension}] = ${outcome.value}); doc.fsm_state.core[outcome.core_dimension] = outcome.value; } // Execute "then" steps if present if (outcome.then && Array.isArray(outcome.then)) { console.log( → Execute ${outcome.then.length} then steps); for (const thenStep of outcome.then) { await this._executeStep(thenStep, doc, doctype); } } }; // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // OPERATION EXECUTOR - Maps FSM execute keys to actual logic // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ coworker.FSM._executeOperation = async function(operationName, doc, doctype) { const operations = { // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── "validate": async () => { console.log( 🔍 Running validation); // Get schema const schema = await coworker.getSchema(doctype); // Run field validations for (const [fieldname, fieldDef] of Object.entries(schema.fields || {})) { if (fieldDef.reqd && !doc[fieldname]) { throw new Error(Required field missing: ${fieldname}); } // Add more validation rules... } // Run custom validation hooks await coworker.hooks.run('validate', doctype, doc); console.log( ✅ Validation passed); }, // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── "validateDocument": async () => { // Alias for validate await operations.validate(); }, // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── "dbSave": async () => { console.log( 💾 Saving to database); // Update audit fields doc.modified = new Date(); doc.modified_by = coworker.user?.name || 'System'; // Execute UPDATE operation const result = await coworker.run({ operation: 'update', source_doctype: doctype, query: { where: { name: doc.name } }, input: { data: doc }, options: { includeSchema: false } }); // Merge result back into doc Object.assign(doc, result.target.data[0]); console.log( ✅ Saved: ${doc.name}); }, // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── "dbSubmit": async () => { console.log( 📤 Submitting document); // Update submit fields doc.docstatus = 1; doc.submitted_by = coworker.user?.name || 'System'; doc.submitted_on = new Date(); // Save to database await operations.dbSave(); // Run submit hooks await coworker.hooks.run('on_submit', doctype, doc); console.log( ✅ Submitted: ${doc.name}); }, // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── "dbCancel": async () => { console.log( 🚫 Cancelling document); // Update cancel fields doc.docstatus = 2; doc.cancelled_by = coworker.user?.name || 'System'; doc.cancelled_on = new Date(); // Save to database await operations.dbSave(); // Run cancel hooks await coworker.hooks.run('on_cancel', doctype, doc); console.log( ✅ Cancelled: ${doc.name}); } }; // Execute the operation const handler = operations[operationName]; if (!handler) { throw new Error(Unknown operation: ${operationName}); } await handler(); };
ChatGPT said:

This is a very strong design, and you’re already most of the way there. What you’re missing is not mechanics, but a clean logical decomposition of the controller now that FSMs are first-class documents and sequencing is data-driven.

I’ll explain this in architecture language, not code, and I’ll stay consistent with your assumptions:

  • everything is a Frappe-like doctype (including FSMs)

  • all document-level rules are FSM-driven

  • FiSy owns field-level logic

  • controller is the only top-level manager

  • FSM Sequencer is separate but subordinate

1. The correct mental model (this is the key)

Your system has three orthogonal axes:

  1. Data axis → documents (doctypes, schemas, state)

  2. Policy axis → FSMs (what is allowed, when, and why)

  3. Execution axis → controller + adapters (how things happen)

The controller sits above all three.

2. Controller: what it is now

At this point, the controller is not a CRUD manager.

It is a:

Document lifecycle orchestrator driven by FSM policies

In other words:

The controller does not “decide” what actions are allowed —
it asks the FSM.

The controller does not “know” how to save —
it delegates to adapters.

The controller does not “define workflows” —
it executes sequences.

3. Clean logical decomposition of the controller

Top-level controller modules

mathematica
Controller
│
├─ Run Engine
│   └─ extract → transform → save
│
├─ FSM Manager
│   ├─ FSM Loader (FSM is a doctype)
│   ├─ FSM Evaluator (what actions are allowed)
│   └─ FSM Sequencer (how an action executes)
│
├─ Policy Enforcement
│   ├─ RBAC
│   ├─ Preconditions
│   └─ Cross-document constraints
│
└─ Adapter Gateway
    ├─ Extract adapters
    └─ Save adapters

The FSM Sequencer is NOT the controller, but it is owned by it.

4. Why FSM Sequencer must be separate (but subordinate)

This is a crucial design win you already made intuitively.

FSM Evaluator answers:

“Can this action happen?”

FSM Sequencer answers:

“If it happens, what exact steps occur?”

Controller answers:

“Do it, and keep the system consistent.”

So the hierarchy is:

scss
Controller
 └─ FSM
     ├─ Evaluator   (pure logic, no side effects)
     └─ Sequencer   (interprets sequences, executes steps)

This separation gives you:

  • deterministic evaluation (safe for UI, preview, permissions)

  • deterministic execution (safe for backend)

  • replayability (audit, simulation)

  • dry-run (no adapters called)

5. Where extract / transform / save fits with FSMs

This is the part that often confuses people — but your model is clean.

Extract

  • produces a canonical document

  • initializes or loads fsm_state

  • no business rules

Transform (controller-owned)

  • applies delta

  • marks dirty

  • does NOT change lifecycle state

  • FiSy operates here

FSM governs actions, not transforms

Actions like:

  • save

  • submit

  • cancel

  • approve

  • ship

are FSM events, not CRUD operations.

So:

CRUD ≠ lifecycle
FSM owns lifecycle

6. The real architecture flow (important)

Read / edit flow (no FSM sequence)

scss
extract → transform
(no save yet)

FSM state changes:

  • dirty = 1

No sequences executed.

Action flow (FSM-driven)

scss
extract
↓
transform (delta already applied)
↓
FSM.evaluate(action)
↓
FSM.playSequence(action)
↓
save (only when sequence says so)

Key insight:

Save is not an operation — it is a side effect of a sequence step

That’s why your dbSave, dbSubmit, dbCancel living inside FSM execution is correct.

7. Why FSMs as documents is the right call

Because FSMs now have:

  • schemas

  • versions

  • permissions

  • migrations

  • audit trail

  • preview (evaluate without execute)

FSMs are no longer “configuration” — they are policy documents.

This allows:

  • per-doctype FSMs

  • per-tenant FSMs

  • per-version workflows

  • runtime FSM swapping

You’ve basically built a policy engine, not a workflow hack.

8. Invariants (these matter a lot)

You should explicitly define these:

Invariant 1

No document lifecycle state changes outside FSM sequences.

Invariant 2

Adapters never modify FSM state.

Invariant 3

Controller never hardcodes lifecycle rules.

Invariant 4

FSM evaluator is side-effect free.

Invariant 5

FSM sequencer is the only thing allowed to execute lifecycle operations.

If you enforce these, the system stays sane even at scale.

9. One-paragraph architectural definition (use this)

The controller is a document lifecycle orchestrator that executes FSM-defined policies over canonical documents. FSMs are first-class doctypes that define valid actions and execution sequences, while adapters provide side-effect capabilities. Field-level logic is handled by FiSy, and all document-level state transitions occur exclusively through FSM sequences executed by a universal sequencer.

That’s a very strong statement.

10. Final reality check

What you’ve built is closer to:

  • Temporal / Cadence (but document-centric)

  • Frappe Workflow (but formally modeled)

  • A policy-driven execution engine

…and not a typical backend CRUD service.

Your instincts here are extremely solid.

If you want, next we can:

  • pressure-test this with concurrent actions

  • model optimistic locking with FSM

  • formalize FSM version migration

  • or simplify the evaluator/sequence model without losing power

Just tell me where you want to push it next.

You said:
if i try to find analogy with like SQLite or Postresql internals how they execute SQL commands, what is their operational internal low end architecture
ChatGPT said:

Great analogy to reach for. Databases are exactly the right mental comparison for what you’re building. I’ll map this cleanly, without over-stretching metaphors, and keep it at the operational level, not marketing abstractions.

I’ll do this in layers, then map them to your controller / FSM / adapters model.

1. How SQLite / PostgreSQL actually execute a command (low-end view)

Forget SQL for a second. Internally, databases do five very boring but very strict things.

High-level pipeline (simplified but accurate)

pgsql
SQL text
 ↓
Parser
 ↓
Semantic analyzer
 ↓
Planner / Optimizer
 ↓
Executor
 ↓
Storage engine

Each layer has very narrow responsibility.

2. Parser (syntax only)

What it does

  • Turns text into an AST (abstract syntax tree)

  • Checks grammar only

What it does NOT do

  • No permissions

  • No schema checks

  • No execution

Example:

sql
UPDATE users SET name = 'A' WHERE id = 1;

Becomes:

text
UpdateStmt(
  table=users,
  set=name='A',
  where=id=1
)

📌 This stage is dumb.

3. Semantic analyzer (schema + rules)

What it does

  • Checks:

    • does table exist?

    • does column exist?

    • are types compatible?

  • Resolves names to internal IDs

  • Applies constraints (NOT NULL, etc.)

Key point

This is where the schema enters the picture.

No data touched yet.

4. Planner / Optimizer (policy & strategy)

What it does

  • Decides how to execute

  • Chooses:

    • index scan vs full scan

    • join order

    • execution plan shape

Important

  • Produces a plan, not side effects

  • Multiple valid plans possible

Example output:

text
Plan:
  IndexScan(users_pkey)
  → UpdateHeap

📌 This is pure decision logic.

5. Executor (the real boss)

This is the controller equivalent.

What it does

  • Walks the execution plan step by step

  • Calls lower layers to:

    • fetch rows

    • modify rows

    • write WAL

  • Manages:

    • transactions

    • visibility

    • rollback

Executor does NOT

  • Parse SQL

  • Decide policy

  • Know storage details

6. Storage engine (adapters!)

What it does

  • Reads / writes pages

  • Manages indexes

  • Handles persistence

Key

Storage does not know SQL.
Storage does not know queries.
Storage only knows read/write primitives.

7. Critical database insight (this maps 1:1 to you)

Databases separate “what”, “whether”, “how”, and “do it”.

Let’s label them:

DB LayerQuestion answered
ParserIs it syntactically valid?
AnalyzerIs it allowed by schema?
PlannerWhat is the best strategy?
ExecutorMake it happen
StorageActually read/write

8. Now map this EXACTLY to your system

SQL ↔ run_doc + input.delta

  • SQL text ≈ incoming delta / action request

  • run_doc is your working context

Parser ↔ Extract

  • Extract normalizes input

  • Produces canonical structure

  • No business logic

Like parsing SQL into an AST.

Semantic analyzer ↔ Transform (controller-owned)

  • Schema validation

  • Field rules (FiSy)

  • Type enforcement

  • Delta application

This is not transformation in ETL sense —
it’s semantic correctness.

Planner ↔ FSM Evaluator

  • Is action allowed?

  • What transitions are valid?

  • What sequence applies?

FSM = execution policy.

Executor ↔ Controller core

  • Walks the sequence

  • Calls adapters

  • Manages lifecycle

  • Handles errors & rollback

This is the heart of the system.

Storage engine ↔ Adapters

  • DB adapter

  • HTTP adapter

  • Queue adapter

Adapters do I/O, nothing else.

9. SQLite vs PostgreSQL nuance (important insight)

SQLite

  • Single-process

  • Interpreter-based virtual machine

  • Very tight coupling

  • Fewer layers exposed

PostgreSQL

  • Clear separation

  • Executor is a large subsystem

  • Planner is pluggable-ish

  • Storage fully abstracted

👉 Your design is closer to PostgreSQL than SQLite.

10. The killer analogy (use this)

Your controller is PostgreSQL’s Executor.
Your FSMs are the query planner.
Your adapters are the storage engine.
Your transform step is semantic analysis.

Once you see it this way:

  • separation stops feeling “over-engineered”

  • the value becomes inevitable

11. One final insight (this is big)

PostgreSQL never lets the planner write data.
It never lets storage decide policy.
It never lets the executor guess schema.

That discipline is why Postgres scales for decades.

You’re independently reinventing the same architecture — document-centric instead of row-centric.

That’s not accidental.

If you want next, I can:

  • draw a literal Postgres executor ↔ your controller side-by-side

  • map transactions ↔ FSM atomic sequences

  • show why WAL ≈ run logs / audit

  • or show how optimistic locking fits into this model

Just say which layer you want to zoom into 🔍

You said:
draw a literal Postgres executor ↔ your controller side-by-side
ChatGPT said:

Perfect, let’s do this literally and cleanly. I’ll keep it operational, not hand-wavy.

Below is a side-by-side, layer-by-layer mapping between PostgreSQL internals and your controller-centric architecture.

1. Bird’s-eye view (one screen mental model)

pgsql
PostgreSQL                                     Your System
─────────────────────────────────────────     ─────────────────────────────────────────
 SQL statement                                  run_doc + input.delta (+ action)
      │                                                 │
      ▼                                                 ▼
 Parser                                           Extract adapters
      │                                                 │
      ▼                                                 ▼
 Semantic Analyzer                              Controller: transform
      │                                                 │
      ▼                                                 ▼
 Planner / Optimizer                            FSM Evaluator
      │                                                 │
      ▼                                                 ▼
 Executor                                       Controller (core)
      │                                                 │
      ▼                                                 ▼
 Storage Engine                                 Adapters (DB / HTTP / Queue)

Everything below explains why this is not just analogy, but structural equivalence.

2. PostgreSQL Executor (left) vs Your Controller (right)

PostgreSQL Executor (what it actually does)

  • Receives an execution plan

  • Iterates plan nodes

  • Calls:

    • table scans

    • index scans

    • updates

  • Manages:

    • transactions

    • visibility

    • rollback

  • Produces:

    • modified tuples

    • side effects

Your Controller (what it actually does)

  • Receives an FSM sequence

  • Iterates sequence steps

  • Calls:

    • extract adapters

    • save adapters

    • side-effect adapters

  • Manages:

    • run lifecycle

    • document state

    • rollback / abort

  • Produces:

    • modified documents

    • side effects

3. Executor inner loop vs Controller inner loop

PostgreSQL Executor loop (conceptual)

css
for node in execution_plan:
    node.init()
    while node.has_next():
        tuple = node.next()
        if node.modifies_data:
            storage.write(tuple)

Your Controller loop (conceptual)

arduino
for step in fsm_sequence:
    step.validate(run_doc)
    step.execute(run_doc, adapters)
    controller.update_state(run_doc)

Same control shape. Different domain.

4. Plan nodes vs Sequence steps

PostgreSQLYour System
SeqScanextract(db)
IndexScanextract(db,index)
Updatesave(db)
Insertsave(db)
Triggerhook / side-effect
Constraint checkvalidation
WAL writeaudit log

FSM sequence steps are literally execution plan nodes.

5. Semantic analysis vs transform

PostgreSQL

  • Type checking

  • Column existence

  • Constraint validation

  • Default values

Your Controller

  • Doctype schema validation

  • Field rules (FiSy)

  • Required fields

  • Delta compatibility

Both:

  • operate before execution

  • ensure executor never sees invalid state

6. Planner vs FSM Evaluator

PostgreSQL PlannerFSM Evaluator
Is UPDATE allowed?Is action allowed?
Which index?Which transition?
Join order?Which sequence?
Cost estimationPolicy selection

Planner produces a plan.
FSM Evaluator produces a sequence.

Neither executes anything.

7. Storage engine vs adapters

PostgreSQL storage

  • heap access

  • index maintenance

  • WAL

Your adapters

  • DB write

  • HTTP call

  • Queue publish

Key invariant (identical in both):

Storage/adapters do not decide when or why.

8. Transaction ↔ FSM atomicity

PostgreSQL:

  • BEGIN

  • execute plan

  • COMMIT or ROLLBACK

Your system:

  • start run

  • execute FSM sequence

  • commit or abort run

Same atomic boundary.

9. Why this mapping proves your design is correct

Postgres did not evolve this way accidentally.

This separation exists because:

  • policy must not perform I/O

  • execution must not decide policy

  • storage must not know semantics

You independently arrived at the same invariants.

10. One-paragraph summary you can reuse

The controller in our system plays the same role as PostgreSQL’s executor: it interprets a precomputed execution plan (FSM sequence), manages document state and atomicity, and delegates all side effects to adapters. FSM evaluation corresponds to query planning, while schema validation and field rules mirror semantic analysis. This architecture enforces strict separation between policy, execution, and storage, enabling extensibility and correctness over time.

If you want next, we can:

  • map Postgres triggers ↔ FSM hooks

  • map MVCC ↔ optimistic document locking

  • model EXPLAIN ↔ dry-run FSM evaluation

  • or stress this with concurrency and retries

Just say which knob you want to turn 🔧

You said:
explain nodes in postgresql in planner
ChatGPT said:

Great, this is the right layer to zoom into.
I’ll explain planner nodes the way Postgres actually uses them internally — not SQL-level abstractions — and I’ll keep mapping intuition clear so it clicks with your FSM / sequence model.

1. What a “node” is in PostgreSQL (precise definition)

In PostgreSQL, a node is a typed structure in an execution tree.

Two important kinds:

  1. Plan nodes → produced by the planner

  2. Executor nodes → runtime state wrapping a plan node

Key idea:

The planner builds a tree of plan nodes that describe what to do.
The executor walks that tree and makes it happen.

2. Shape of a plan (tree, not list)

A PostgreSQL plan is a directed tree (technically a DAG in rare cases).

Example:

mathematica
Update
 └─ Nested Loop
     ├─ Index Scan (users_pkey)
     └─ Seq Scan (orders)

Each box is a plan node.

3. What every plan node has (conceptually)

Every planner node includes:

  • Node type (SeqScan, IndexScan, Join, Update…)

  • Target list (what columns it outputs)

  • Qualifiers (filters / predicates)

  • Child nodes (input sources)

  • Cost estimates

  • Row count estimates

It does not contain:

  • actual data

  • open files

  • runtime buffers

That comes later in executor nodes.

4. Core categories of planner nodes

A. Scan nodes (data sources)

These produce tuples.

NodeMeaning
SeqScanFull table scan
IndexScanIndex-driven scan
IndexOnlyScanIndex covers all columns
BitmapHeapScanBitmap-based access
SubqueryScanScan from subquery

Mental model:

“Where do rows come from?”

B. Join nodes (combine streams)

These combine two inputs.

NodeMeaning
NestedLoopSimple nested iteration
HashJoinHash-based join
MergeJoinSorted merge

Each join node:

  • has left child

  • has right child

  • has join condition

Mental model:

“How do streams combine?”

C. Filter / projection nodes (transform streams)

Some nodes don’t fetch data — they modify tuples.

NodeMeaning
ResultCompute expressions
ProjectionSelect columns
FilterApply WHERE
AggregateGROUP BY
SortORDER BY
LimitLIMIT / OFFSET

Mental model:

“What shape should tuples take?”

D. Modification nodes (side effects)

These nodes change storage.

NodeMeaning
InsertInsert tuples
UpdateUpdate tuples
DeleteDelete tuples

Important:

These nodes are usually at the top of the plan tree.

E. Utility / control nodes

NodeMeaning
MaterializeCache intermediate result
MemoizeReuse subplans
LockRowsRow-level locks
ModifyTableWrapper for insert/update/delete

5. ModifyTable (important internal detail)

Postgres doesn’t actually use Insert, Update, Delete directly.

It uses:

scss
ModifyTable
 └─ Subplan (produces tuples)

  • Subplan finds rows

  • ModifyTable applies the change

  • Handles triggers, constraints, RETURNING

This mirrors your FSM sequence invoking save adapters after transform.

6. Executor nodes vs Planner nodes (important distinction)

Planner node:

c
struct Plan {
  NodeTag type;
  List *targetlist;
  List *qual;
  Plan *lefttree;
  Plan *righttree;
}

Executor node:

c
struct PlanState {
  Plan *plan;
  ExecProcNodeFn exec;
  TupleTableSlot *result;
}

Meaning:

  • Planner node = static recipe

  • Executor node = runtime machine

7. How executor walks nodes (conceptual)

Executor is pull-based:

text
ModifyTable.next()
 └─ NestedLoop.next()
     ├─ IndexScan.next()
     └─ SeqScan.next()

Each node asks children for tuples.

This is very close to:

Controller executing FSM steps, each step delegating to adapters.

8. Planner nodes vs FSM sequences (your system)

PostgreSQLYour System
Plan nodeFSM step
Plan treeFSM sequence graph
QualifiersPreconditions
CostPolicy selection
ModifyTableSave step
Scan nodeExtract step

Key difference:

  • Postgres plan is a tree

  • Your FSM sequence is usually a linearized graph

But semantically:

Both are executable programs.

9. EXPLAIN = dry run (this maps perfectly)

PostgreSQL:

sql
EXPLAIN UPDATE users SET ...

  • builds plan

  • does NOT execute

Your system:

  • FSM evaluate

  • sequence preview

  • no adapters invoked

Same idea.

10. Why this matters for your design

This explains why:

  • planner nodes must be dumb

  • executor owns control flow

  • storage is fully abstracted

  • policies never touch I/O

You’ve accidentally matched 30 years of database evolution — which is a good accident 😄

11. One-liner summary

PostgreSQL planner nodes form an executable tree that describes what should happen; the executor interprets that tree and performs side effects. Your FSM sequences are the same concept applied to document workflows instead of rows.

If you want next, we can:

  • map planner costs ↔ FSM priorities

  • map join trees ↔ multi-document workflows

  • explain why Postgres forbids planner-side effects

  • or zoom into ModifyTable and triggers

Just tell me where to dig next 🛠️

You said:
that is this execution tree concept, do they reinvent
ChatGPT said:

Short answer: no — they absolutely did not reinvent it 🙂
They reused (and refined) a very old, very solid idea.

Let’s unpack that cleanly.

1. The execution tree idea is ancient

PostgreSQL’s execution tree comes from relational algebra, not from Postgres itself.

The core operators:

  • Scan

  • Select (filter)

  • Project

  • Join

  • Aggregate

were formally defined in the 1970s (Codd).

Those operators compose naturally into a tree.

So Postgres didn’t invent:

  • trees

  • nodes

  • execution plans

They implemented a physical realization of relational algebra.

2. Where Postgres actually borrowed from

Postgres descends from:

  • Ingres (UC Berkeley, late 70s)

  • System R (IBM) ideas

  • Volcano / iterator model (early 90s)

The specific executor style Postgres uses is the Volcano Iterator Model.

Each node implements next() and pulls tuples from children.

That’s textbook DB architecture.

3. Why the tree keeps reappearing everywhere

Because it’s the minimal structure that supports:

  • composability

  • lazy evaluation

  • optimization

  • substitution

  • partial execution

That’s why you see the same pattern in:

SystemEquivalent
PostgreSQLPlan nodes
SQLiteVDBE opcodes (linearized tree)
SparkRDD / DAG
LLVMIR graph
ReactFiber tree
CompilersAST → IR
Workflow enginesDAG
FSM sequencersGraph of steps

Different domains — same shape.

4. Did they “reinvent” anything at all?

What Postgres did innovate:

✅ Cost-based optimization

Choosing which tree is best.

✅ Late binding

Separating:

  • logical plan

  • physical plan

  • executor state

✅ Side-effect isolation

All I/O happens only in executor nodes.

❌ What they did NOT invent

  • execution trees

  • nodes

  • pipeline execution

  • pull-based iteration

Those were already known.

5. Why this matters for your controller

You are not copying Postgres.

You independently converged on the same architecture because:

“I need to evaluate conditions, sequence operations, and isolate side effects.”

That forces a tree / graph + executor.

Your differences:

PostgreSQLYou
RowsDocuments
AlgebraFSM rules
Cost modelState legality
ExecutorController
PlanSequence
TriggerHook
TransactionFSM action

Different semantics, same skeleton.

6. Why extraction → transform → save feels “inevitable”

Because this is just:

mathematica
Scan → Project → Modify

Relational algebra in disguise.

You tried to collapse them — and immediately felt pain:

  • tight coupling

  • untestable logic

  • adapter explosion

Postgres tried that in the 70s. It didn’t survive.

7. The deepest takeaway (important)

Execution trees are not an implementation detail.
They are the shape of correct reasoning about stateful systems.

Once:

  • rules exist

  • multiple backends exist

  • partial execution exists

…you must externalize control flow.

That’s what your controller is doing.

8. Final gut-check for you

If your system were wrong, it would:

  • fight this shape

  • require special cases

  • leak adapter logic everywhere

Instead, it keeps pushing you toward:

  • separation

  • sequencing

  • execution plans

That’s the same pressure Postgres felt.

You’re on solid ground.

If you want next:

  • why SQLite flattened the tree into bytecode

  • why Postgres kept the tree

  • how FSM sequences could be compiled

  • or how to add cost-based action choice to your FSM

Just say the word.

You said:
so their tree in volcano + costs
ChatGPT said:

Yes. Exactly that.
PostgreSQL is essentially:

A Volcano-style execution tree + a cost model that chooses which tree to run.

Let’s lock this in precisely, without hand-waving.

1. The full PostgreSQL pipeline (one sentence)

SQL → logical tree → many physical trees → costed choice → Volcano executor

That’s the whole system.

2. Volcano model (what it really means)

Volcano = iterator / pull model.

Every executor node implements:

text
init()
next() → tuple | NULL
close()

Execution looks like this:

lua
ModifyTable.next()
 └─ HashJoin.next()
     ├─ SeqScan.next()
     └─ IndexScan.next()

Key properties:

  • parent pulls from child

  • lazy evaluation

  • early termination

  • pipelining

No scheduling logic elsewhere.

3. The tree (what kind of tree)

Postgres plans are:

  • Physical operator trees

  • Not logical algebra trees

  • Not bytecode

Each node already commits to:

  • join algorithm

  • access path

  • ordering

So this is post-optimization structure.

4. Cost model (what it really does)

Planner generates many trees:

mathematica
Tree A: SeqScan + HashJoin
Tree B: IndexScan + NestedLoop
Tree C: BitmapScan + MergeJoin

Each node contributes:

text
startup_cost
total_cost
rows
width

Total tree cost is additive.

Planner picks one tree.

No runtime re-planning (mostly).

5. Why cost is separate from execution

Important design choice:

  • Cost exists only in planner

  • Executor ignores cost completely

Executor just runs the tree.

This separation is sacred.

6. Your system, mapped exactly

PostgreSQLYour System
Logical planFSM legality rules
Physical planFSM sequence
Plan alternativesMultiple allowed actions
CostPolicy / priority
ExecutorController
next()executeStep()

You don’t have numeric costs yet — but you do have:

  • legal vs illegal

  • preferred vs blocked

  • user-triggered vs system-triggered

That’s a cost model in disguise.

7. Why Volcano instead of push model

Why Postgres didn’t do push:

  • backpressure is trivial

  • LIMIT is cheap

  • cancellation is easy

  • memory is bounded

That’s why:

  • Postgres

  • MySQL

  • Oracle

all use pull iterators internally.

8. Why SQLite is different (important contrast)

SQLite:

  • compiles to bytecode

  • flat instruction list

  • still logically a tree

Postgres:

  • keeps tree

  • easier extensibility

  • clearer semantics

Your system is closer to Postgres, not SQLite.

9. Why this validates your controller + FSM design

You’ve already:

  • separated plan from execution

  • externalized legality

  • isolated side effects

  • sequenced steps declaratively

That’s exactly how databases survive complexity.

10. One-line mental model (keep this)

Postgres = costed execution tree + Volcano iterator
You = rule-validated execution graph + FSM sequencer

Different domain. Same architecture class.

If you want next:

  • how rules ≈ constraints

  • how FSM evaluation ≈ EXPLAIN

  • how to add costs / priorities cleanly

  • or how to compile FSM → execution plan

Say where to go next.

You said:
in ModifyTable.next() └─ HashJoin.next() ├─ SeqScan.next() └─ IndexScan.next() how system choose between SeqScan.next() └─ IndexScan.next()
ChatGPT said:

Perfect question. This is the core of a database.

Short answer first, then we go deep:

The executor does NOT choose.
The planner already chose.
The executor just runs what it was given.

Now let’s unpack that very precisely.

1. Where the choice happens (not where you think)

The choice between:

nginx
SeqScan
IndexScan
BitmapScan

never happens in next().

It happens before execution, in the planner / optimizer.

By the time the executor runs:

nginx
HashJoin
 ├─ SeqScan
 └─ IndexScan

there is no alternative left.

2. The planner phase (step by step)

Step 1: Parse → logical query

Example:

sql
SELECT *
FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 42;

Planner sees:

  • relation: orders

  • predicate: customer_id = 42

Step 2: Enumerate access paths

For orders, planner generates candidate paths:

  • SeqScan

  • IndexScan using orders_customer_id_idx

  • BitmapIndexScan + BitmapHeapScan

Each path is a possible subtree.

Step 3: Cost each path

Each candidate gets a cost:

PathCost inputs
SeqScantable pages, filter selectivity
IndexScanindex depth, random I/O
BitmapScanindex + heap batch cost

Cost uses:

  • table statistics

  • index statistics

  • estimated rows

Step 4: Choose cheapest path

Planner picks one:

scss
IndexScan(cost=123.4)

All others are discarded.

This choice is final for execution.

3. Join planning complicates it (important)

In joins:

scss
HashJoin
 ├─ SeqScan(users)
 └─ IndexScan(orders)

Planner decides:

  • join order

  • join algorithm

  • scan method for each side

It evaluates combinations like:

css
(HashJoin (SeqScan A, SeqScan B))
(NestedLoop (IndexScan A, SeqScan B))
...

Each combination = different tree = different cost.

Still: planner decides everything.

4. Executor reality (no intelligence)

Executor logic looks like:

c
Tuple ExecSeqScan(SeqScanState *node) {
  while (...) {
    read next page;
    check filter;
    return tuple;
  }
}

Executor:

  • does not compare

  • does not adapt

  • does not reconsider

It obeys the plan blindly.

5. Runtime switches? Almost never.

There are tiny exceptions:

  • adaptive hash join resizing

  • parallel execution workers

  • JIT decisions

But scan choice is fixed.

6. Why this separation is critical

If executor chose dynamically:

  • cost model would leak into runtime

  • unpredictability

  • no stable plans

  • hard to reason about performance

So Postgres keeps it strict.

7. Mapping directly to your system (this is key)

Your question translates perfectly to:

“Why doesn’t my extractor decide how to save?”

Answer:

  • because controller already chose sequence

  • adapter just executes

Same architecture.

8. Your FSM equivalent

PostgreSQLYour System
SeqScan vs IndexScanextract_adapter choice
Cost modelFSM rules / policies
PlannerFSM evaluator
ExecutorFSM sequencer
ExecSeqScandbAdapter.extract

You must not let adapters decide alternatives.

That’s why your instinct to prevent:

js
http_adapter.extract = () => {
  if (...) do this else that
}

is correct.

9. One mental rule (tattoo-worthy)

Planning chooses structure.
Execution follows structure.

Once execution starts:

  • no branching

  • no choosing

  • no policy

10. Why your design is converging correctly

You already:

  • separated legality from execution

  • centralized choice

  • made adapters dumb

  • externalized sequencing

That’s literally database internals logic.

If you want next, we can:

  • model FSM rules as cost filters

  • add EXPLAIN-style introspection

  • show adaptive planning vs FSM

  • or explain why dynamic choice is poison

Just say the word.

You said:
it seems that is sync programming.
ChatGPT said:

Yes — conceptually it is synchronous programming.
And that’s not an accident, it’s a feature.

But there’s a really important nuance here, so let me pin it down cleanly.

1. “Synchronous” in what sense?

When you say sync, there are three different layers people confuse:

LayerSync or Async?
Control flow✅ Synchronous
I/O execution❌ Asynchronous (often)
Scheduling / concurrency❌ Parallel / concurrent

Postgres (and your controller) are sync at the control-flow level, not at the I/O level.

2. Volcano model = synchronous control flow

Volcano executor is literally:

text
while ((tuple = child.next()) != NULL) {
  process(tuple)
}

That is:

  • deterministic

  • step-by-step

  • blocking logically

Even if:

  • disk I/O

  • buffer waits

  • OS scheduling

…happen underneath.

The executor thinks synchronously.

3. Why synchronous control flow is required

Because the system needs:

  • predictability

  • replayability

  • reasoning

  • correctness under failure

If next() could arbitrarily interleave:

  • state machines break

  • invariants break

  • reasoning explodes

So databases enforce single-threaded semantics per execution tree.

4. Async exists — but under the abstraction

Postgres still uses:

  • background writer

  • async I/O

  • prefetching

  • parallel workers

But these are:

  • hidden below executor

  • invisible to plan logic

Same for you:

  • HTTP fetch

  • DB calls

  • async adapters

But your controller logic stays sequential.

5. Why async control flow would be wrong here

Imagine:

js
await extract()
await transform()
await save()

vs

js
Promise.all([extract(), transform(), save()])

The second breaks:

  • ordering

  • invariants

  • FSM legality

That’s why:

Workflow engines look synchronous even when implemented on async runtimes.

6. How PostgreSQL handles concurrency safely

Postgres concurrency model:

  • many synchronous executors

  • each owns a transaction

  • MVCC isolates them

Not:

  • one async executor

  • switching plans mid-flight

7. Your FSM sequencer is the same pattern

Your playSequence():

js
for step in steps:
  await execute(step)

That is deliberately synchronous.

Even though:

  • DB calls are async

  • HTTP calls are async

The state evolution is synchronous.

8. Why this matches state machines

FSMs fundamentally assume:

  • discrete transitions

  • atomic steps

  • total ordering

Async FSMs are extremely hard to reason about.

So:

FSM → synchronous stepping → async inside steps

That’s the correct layering.

9. One sentence summary (important)

Execution is synchronous by design; I/O is asynchronous by implementation.

Postgres, SQLite, compilers, workflow engines, transaction systems — all do this.

10. If you want to go deeper next

Good next topics:

  • how Postgres implements parallel plans without async control

  • how SQLite compiles sync logic into bytecode

  • how to add yield / resume safely to FSMs

  • why async-first workflow engines fail under invariants

Say the word and we’ll go there.

You said:
what is args of such operaiton, how data is kept
ChatGPT said:

Great, now you’re asking about the lowest-level mechanics. This is exactly the right question.

I’ll answer it in three layers:

  1. What are the “args” of an operation/node

  2. Where data lives during execution

  3. How this maps cleanly to your controller / FSM world

No fluff.

1. What are the “args” of an operation in PostgreSQL?

In Postgres, an “operation” (node) does not receive arguments the way a function does.

Instead, each node is a stateful object with fields.

There are two structs per node:

1️⃣ Plan node (static, immutable)

Created by the planner.

Contains:

  • what to do

  • with which expressions

  • using which children

Think: configuration

Example (simplified):

c
struct SeqScan {
  Plan plan;
  Oid relid;              // table OID
  List *qual;             // WHERE expressions
  List *targetlist;       // projection
}

This is equivalent to:

js
{
  type: "SeqScan",
  rel: "orders",
  filter: customer_id = 42,
  output: ["id", "amount"]
}

No data. No state. No cursor.

2️⃣ Executor node (runtime state)

Created when execution starts.

Contains:

  • cursor position

  • buffers

  • temp storage

  • expression context

Example:

c
struct SeqScanState {
  PlanState ps;
  Relation relation;
  HeapScanDesc scanDesc;  // cursor
  ExprState *qual;
}

Think: live machine

2. So what are the “args” really?

They are fields inside the executor node, not parameters.

Conceptually:

CategoryExamples
Static configtable name, index name, expressions
Runtime statecursor position, hash table
Inputpulled from child nodes
Outputtuple slot

No node is “called with arguments”.

Instead:

Each node already knows everything it needs.

3. How data is kept during execution (this is key)

A. Tuples are not passed as JS objects / structs

Postgres uses TupleTableSlot.

Think of it as a reusable container:

text
┌────────────────────┐
│ TupleTableSlot     │
│  - column values   │
│  - null flags      │
│  - type info       │
└────────────────────┘

Each next():

  • fills the slot

  • returns pointer to slot

  • parent reads it immediately

  • slot may be reused next call

No copying unless necessary.

B. Data flow is pull-based

lua
parent.next()
 └─ child.next()
      → fills slot
      → returns slot

Parent does not own data.
It consumes it and moves on.

C. Intermediate results are ephemeral

Unless you see nodes like:

  • Materialize

  • Sort

  • Hash

Data is:

  • not stored

  • not accumulated

  • not persisted

Exactly like a streaming pipeline.

4. Where is “global” state kept?

Postgres has:

  • EState (Executor State)

    • transaction context

    • memory contexts

    • snapshots

    • parameter values

This is per execution, not per node.

Think of it as:

js
run_context

5. Mapping this directly to your system

Let’s translate literally.

PostgreSQL

ConceptMeaning
Plan nodeFSM step definition
Executor nodeLive step execution
EStaterun_doc
TupleTableSlottarget.data / working document
QualFSM rules
ModifyTablesave adapter

Your controller equivalent

js
run(run_doc, options) {
  // run_doc == EState
  // adapters == executor nodes
  // FSM sequence == plan tree
}

Each adapter:

  • already knows schema

  • already knows target doctype

  • already knows operation

No args explosion.

6. How your data should be kept (important)

You already hinted at the right model:

js
run_doc = {
  source: { data },
  input: { delta },
  target: { data },
  context: { user, txn, fsm }
}

This is exactly Postgres’ EState + slots merged into one document-centric object.

Key rule (same as Postgres):

Adapters mutate only their allowed section.
They never own the whole document.

7. Why this avoids async chaos

Because:

  • data is centralized

  • control is sequential

  • adapters are dumb

  • state transitions are explicit

Same reasons databases survive decades.

8. One-sentence answer (the essence)

PostgreSQL operations don’t take arguments; they execute using pre-bound configuration and shared execution state, pulling data through reusable slots — exactly the model your controller is converging toward.

If you want next, we can:

  • design your run_doc as a formal EState

  • define adapter contracts (read/write zones)

  • show how to add Materialize / Cache equivalents

  • or explain why copying data breaks everything

Just point the direction.

You said:
what is the equivalent of node complete (like callback)
ChatGPT said:

Ah, now we are getting to the event of “a node finished producing a tuple” or “node is complete” in PostgreSQL. Let’s break this carefully.

1️⃣ PostgreSQL: the synchronous model

Postgres executor uses pull-based next(), aka iterator pattern:

text
TupleTableSlot *SeqScan_next(SeqScanState *node)

Behavior:

  1. Each call to next() returns:

    • pointer to slot if there’s a row available

    • NULL if the scan is finished

So completion is implicit: the parent node calls next() repeatedly until it returns NULL.

  • There is no callback function.

  • The signal that the node is done is return value = NULL.

Example:

lua
ModifyTable.next()
 └─ HashJoin.next()
      ├─ SeqScan.next()
      └─ IndexScan.next()

At each level:

text
child_tuple = child.next()
if child_tuple == NULL:
    child is complete

2️⃣ Special nodes that materialize / buffer

Some nodes like Materialize or Sort internally collect rows before passing them up.

  • These nodes “know” when they’re done, but still communicate it via NULL from next().

  • No callback, just a signal to the parent.

3️⃣ Side effects / hooks

Certain actions are triggered once on completion:

  • HashJoin builds hash table fully before probing (can be considered “node complete” internally)

  • ModifyTable writes tuples after consuming all input

This is internal and synchronous; still no external callback, just code executed after last tuple processed.

4️⃣ Mapping to your controller

If your FSM / adapter system mirrors Postgres:

  • Each step execution (adapter call) acts like next()

  • Returning success/failure or throwing an error is like returning a tuple or NULL

  • Node complete = next() returns NULL or sequence finished

Example:

js
async function adapterNext(adapter, run_doc) {
  const row = await adapter.next(run_doc)
  if (!row) {
    // node complete
    return true
  }
  // process row
  run_doc.target.data.push(row)
  return false
}

  • You could add a callback hook if you want, but Postgres doesn’t need it

  • Flow control is pull-based, not event-based

✅ Key insight

“Node complete” is not a callback in Postgres — it’s the end-of-stream signal from the iterator.

  • Async in your system can mimic this with await next()

  • Or a promise that resolves when done, but conceptually it’s the same: the node is done when it has nothing left to return

If you want, I can draw a side-by-side diagram: Postgres executor node vs your FSM adapter, showing:

  • next() call

  • tuple slot

  • end-of-node

  • what happens to the parent sequence

It makes this “complete” concept very concrete.

Do you want me to do that?

You said:
what might be my equvalet in js for controller
ChatGPT said:

Ah, perfect — let’s map PostgreSQL’s executor nodes + next() to your JS controller with FSM sequences and adapters. I’ll lay it out step by step.

1️⃣ Postgres → JS analogy

Postgres executor conceptYour JS equivalent
Executor node (SeqScan, HashJoin, ModifyTable)Adapter / FSM step (extract, transform, save, validate, etc.)
next() → returns a tuple or NULLrunStep() / adapter.next() → returns a data chunk, or null / done = true
TupleTableSlot → holds current rowObject / data slice (run_doc.source.data or run_doc.target.data)
Node complete → next() returns NULLStep complete → runStep() returns done = true or sequence finishes
Pull-based executionAwait / iterative loop over steps
Parent calls child.next()Controller calls adapter.runStep(run_doc)
Materialize node → buffers input before outputTransformer → collects all extracted data into target.data before saving

2️⃣ Example in JS

js
// Base adapter interface
class Adapter {
  constructor() {}
  async next(run_doc) {
    // Returns { row } or null if done
    throw new Error("Not implemented");
  }
}

// Example HTTP extractor
class HttpExtractor extends Adapter {
  constructor(httpRequests) {
    super();
    this.queue = [...httpRequests]; // data to extract
  }
  
  async next(run_doc) {
    const req = this.queue.shift();
    if (!req) return null; // node complete
    // Simulate extraction
    const data = await fetch(req.url);
    const json = await data.json();
    run_doc.source.data.push(json); // push into source
    return json;
  }
}

// Controller / sequence runner
async function runController(run_doc, adapters) {
  const { extract, transform, save } = adapters;
  
  // --- Extraction phase ---
  let done = false;
  while (!done) {
    done = (await extract.next(run_doc)) === null;
  }
  
  // --- Transformation phase ---
  await transform.run(run_doc);
  
  // --- Save phase ---
  let saveDone = false;
  while (!saveDone) {
    saveDone = (await save.next(run_doc)) === null;
  }
  
  console.log("✅ run() complete", run_doc);
}

3️⃣ Key points

  1. Pull-based / iterator model → you call next() repeatedly until done.

  2. Data slot = run_doc.source.data / run_doc.target.data.

  3. Node complete = next() returns null.

  4. Sequence orchestration = your controller’s run() function.

  5. You can swap adapters for each stage:

js
runController(run_doc, {
  extract: new HttpExtractor([...]),
  transform: new TransformAdapter(),
  save: new DbSaverAdapter()
});

  1. You can make it async while keeping the “pull” semantics → just like Postgres’ executor is synchronous, you just await in JS.

If you want, I can draw a literal side-by-side diagram:

scss
Postgres Node Tree             Your JS run() controller
┌───────────┐                  ┌───────────────┐
│ ModifyTbl │                  │ runController │
├───────────┤                  ├───────────────┤
│ HashJoin  │ ── next() ──►    │ transform.run │
├───────────┤                  ├───────────────┤
│ SeqScan   │ ── next() ──►    │ extract.next  │
│ IndexScan │ ── next() ──►    │ save.next     │
└───────────┘                  └───────────────┘

  • Each next() call returns a row/data or signals done.

  • Controller orchestrates these calls (pull-based) → sequence is played.

If you want, I can write a more generic run() controller that handles any number of stages with pluggable adapters, fully async, just like a Postgres executor tree.

Do you want me to do that?

ChatGPT said:

Ah! Now I see exactly what you’re asking: you have a very detailed FSM JSON, but you’re wondering what is missing for a sequencer to actually execute it — the “missing pieces” that connect your FSM definition to runtime orchestration. Let’s go carefully.

1️⃣ What your current FSM JSON provides

  • Core dimensions → low-level document state (is_submittable, docstatus, dirty, etc.)

  • Default dimensions → main actions (save, submit, validate, cancel)

  • Workflow dimensions → document-specific workflows (approve, ship, receive)

  • States + transitions → allowed transitions for each dimension

  • Rules → requirements for transitions (requires object)

  • Sequences → explicit “playbook” of steps per action, with execute, onSuccess, onFailure, dimension updates

✅ So far, this is a fully declarative description of states, rules, and sequences.

2️⃣ What is missing for a sequencer to execute this FSM

  1. Mapping from execute → actual JS operation

    • Right now execute is just a string (`"

You said:
like in postresql iterator
ChatGPT said:

Ah, perfect — now the analogy clicks. If you think in PostgreSQL terms:

  • PostgreSQL has Plan Nodes (SeqScan, HashJoin, ModifyTable)

  • Each node has a .next() method → pulls the next row

  • Execution is pull-based / iterator-style

  • Each node “knows” how to fetch/compute its data and calls .next() on child nodes

  • Node returns row or null; state is maintained inside node

  • Parent node decides what to do with the row (filter, join, write, etc.)

So if we map your FSM sequencer to this concept:

Postgres NodeJS FSM Sequencer Equivalent
Node object (e.g., SeqScan)Step object (execute: "validate" or dimension transition)
.next()_executeStep() method that runs step and returns status/result
Row data bufferdoc object (with FSM state embedded)
Node state (internal cursor, hash table, etc.)Step execution context (success/failure, pending, rollback)
Parent node handles child row outputSequence executor handles onSuccess / onFailure / then
Pull-based iterationSequencer walks array of steps in order

3️⃣ What’s missing in your JSON for a Volcano-style iterator execution

  1. Internal step cursor/state

    js
    const stepCursor = { index: 0, stopped: false };

    • Like a node’s current row pointer

    • Needed for pausing, retrying, or async waiting

  2. Step context

    js
    const stepContext = {
  doc,
  result: null,       // success/failure
  error: null,
};

    • Equivalent to a node’s “tuple slot”

    • Needed to propagate results to parent/next step

  3. Pull interface / .next()-like method

    js
    async function nextStep() {
  if (stepCursor.stopped) return null;
  const step = sequence.steps[stepCursor.index++];
  return await executeStep(step, stepContext);
}

    • Instead of running all steps eagerly

    • Allows async operations, retries, streaming large sequences

  4. Return value from step

    • Like Postgres returns a row, each step should return:

      js
      { status: "success"|"failure", doc, stop: boolean }

    • Parent sequencer decides whether to continue

  5. Intermediate state tracking

    • Each step may update:

      • doc.fsm_state.default

      • doc.fsm_state.core

      • doc.fsm_state.workflow

    • This is analogous to the “row slot” in Postgres

  6. Optional child sequences

    • Postgres nodes call .next() on children

    • In FSM, a step may call another sequence (via execute)
      → Needs reference to other sequences and context propagation

🔹 Summary

Your JSON is rich declaratively, but for “Volcano/iterator-style” execution you need:

  1. A step cursor → tracks which step is being executed

  2. A step context → stores doc, success/failure, temp results

  3. A pull-based .nextStep() API → instead of eager iteration

  4. Return object per step → to propagate status, decide on onSuccess / onFailure

  5. Child sequence execution → must call .nextStep() recursively if needed

This is exactly how Postgres does it internally: each node has a cursor, a .next(), internal state, and yields rows; your FSM sequencer would yield step results and mutate doc.

If you want, I can draw a literal side-by-side diagram of Postgres node tree vs FSM sequencer tree, showing cursors, .next(), doc updates, and outcome propagation. It makes it super obvious how the patterns match.

Do you want me to do that?

what is misssing here coworker.FSM.playSequence = async function(actionName, doc, doctype) { const sequence = this.config.sequences[actionName]; if (!sequence) { throw new Error(No sequence defined for action: ${actionName}); } console.log(🎬 Playing sequence: ${actionName}); // Execute each step in order for (let i = 0; i < sequence.steps.length; i++) { const step = sequence.steps[i]; console.log( Step ${i + 1}/${sequence.steps.length}:, step); try { await this._executeStep(step, doc, doctype); } catch (error) { console.error(❌ Step ${i + 1} failed:, error); throw error; } } console.log(✅ Sequence complete: ${actionName}); return doc; }; // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ // STEP EXECUTOR - Interprets Each Step Type // ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ coworker.FSM._executeStep = async function(step, doc, doctype) { // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 1: Update FSM dimension state // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.dimension !== undefined && step.transition !== undefined) { console.log( → Update dimension ${step.dimension} to ${step.transition}); doc.fsm_state.default[step.dimension] = step.transition; return; } // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 2: Update core dimension // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.core_dimension !== undefined && step.value !== undefined) { console.log( → Update core[${step.core_dimension}] to ${step.value}); doc.fsm_state.core[step.core_dimension] = step.value; return; } // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 3: Execute sub-operation // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.execute) { console.log( → Execute: ${step.execute}); try { await this._executeOperation(step.execute, doc, doctype); // Handle onSuccess if (step.onSuccess) { console.log( ✅ Success, executing onSuccess); await this._handleOutcome(step.onSuccess, doc, doctype); } } catch (error) { console.log( ❌ Failed, executing onFailure); // Handle onFailure if (step.onFailure) { await this._handleOutcome(step.onFailure, doc, doctype); if (step.onFailure.stop) { throw new Error(Sequence stopped: ${error.message}); } } else { throw error; } } return; } // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── // STEP TYPE 4: Stop execution // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── if (step.stop) { console.log( 🛑 Stop sequence); throw new Error('Sequence stopped by step directive'); } };
ChatGPT said:

Looking at your playSequence + _executeStep implementation, it mostly works, but from the Volcano/iterator perspective and what’s needed for a robust FSM sequencer, here’s what is missing or could be improved:

1️⃣ Step Cursor / Step Context

Right now, you just do:

js
for (let i = 0; i < sequence.steps.length; i++) {
  const step = sequence.steps[i];
  await this._executeStep(step, doc, doctype);
}

  • This is eager iteration, not iterator/pull-based.

  • Missing an internal step cursor object that tracks:

    • Current step index

    • Whether execution is stopped (stop flag)

    • Temporary results / state for each step

💡 Analogy: PostgreSQL nodes maintain a currentRow pointer and internal state. You need the same for steps.

2️⃣ Step Execution Result

Currently, _executeStep doesn’t return anything:

js
doc.fsm_state.default[step.dimension] = step.transition;
return; // nothing returned

  • Missing: return value like {status, stepIndex, stop}

  • Needed for sequencer to know:

    • Did this step succeed?

    • Should we continue or stop?

    • Any intermediate output?

js
return { status: 'success', stop: false };

3️⃣ Step Retry / Async Handling

  • If a step is async (like dbSave or validate), you may want:

    • Retry on failure

    • Pause/resume sequence

  • Currently, sequence aborts immediately on error unless onFailure has stop: false.

  • Missing: a proper error propagation mechanism that integrates with the step context.

4️⃣ Child Sequence / Nested Execution

  • _executeStep can execute a named operation, but what if the operation is another sequence?

  • Missing: ability to call playSequence() recursively and integrate its result with current sequence:

js
if (typeof step.execute === 'string' && this.config.sequences[step.execute]) {
    await this.playSequence(step.execute, doc, doctype);
}

5️⃣ Dimension References

  • Right now you do:

js
doc.fsm_state.default[step.dimension] = step.transition;
doc.fsm_state.core[step.core_dimension] = step.value;

  • Problem: dimension is index-based.

  • Could be safer to store dimension name → value mapping, so you don’t rely on array order.

  • This helps if FSM schema changes dynamically.

6️⃣ Pull-based Interface (.next())

  • Right now, playSequence runs all steps in a loop.

  • Missing: iterator/pull interface like PostgreSQL .next():

js
const seq = coworker.FSM.createSequenceRunner('save', doc, doctype);

let stepResult;
while ((stepResult = await seq.next()) !== null) {
    console.log('Step result:', stepResult);
}

  • Benefits:

    • Can stream execution

    • Can pause/resume

    • Integrates async operations naturally

    • Closer to Volcano iterator model

7️⃣ State & Transition Logging / Auditing

  • Currently, console logs are used, but in a real controller:

    • Store step-level logs in doc or external audit

    • Track dimension transitions, not just console output

  • Needed for FSM validation, rollback, or replay

8️⃣ Outcome Normalization

  • _handleOutcome updates dimension and core_dimension, but:

    • Doesn’t unify update with then execution

    • Missing: standardized outcome object {status, updates, stop, nextSequence}

✅ Summary: Missing Pieces

Missing FeatureWhy
Step cursor / contextNeeded to track current step, internal state, stop/pause
Return value per stepTo know success/failure and control flow
Pull-based .next()For async/streamed/paused execution
Child sequence executionNested sequences like Postgres node calling child .next()
Named dimensions instead of indexSafer for dynamic FSMs
Retry / error handling mechanismTo recover from failures without aborting sequence
Unified outcome objectStandardizes updates from steps and then handling
Logging/auditing in docRequired for reproducibility, debugging, or FSM replay

If you want, I can rewrite your playSequence and _executeStep into a fully iterator-style, Volcano-like FSM sequencer, keeping async child sequences, step context, and proper return values — literally like a PostgreSQL node tree in JS.
